lines.st:
'A Fangshi from afar says there is a great mountain on the northern border called Jing, and atop it sits a mystical stone called Wa.

'Thousands of meters wide and tall, shaped like a jewel, the color of crystal...

'It shines with a brilliant blaze, and a luster that never wanes...'

'It cuts both metal and stone, brings forth all things with methods unknowable, with principles unfathomable...'


'Obtain this miraculous object, and perhaps it will help Yan face the coming calamity.'
'I don't get it, sir. We sacrificed so much for this, yet you intend to hand it over to the House of Yan?'
'That man started the war in the first place, and sent thousands to perish. Why would you aid the despot?'
'We wish to free the masses from suffering, not to aid a single ruler... Fate shall play its hand in time; no one person controls it...'
'I only pray for an early end to the strife, that the people would no longer suffer its torments...'

lines.x: Ah... yes, yes...!
This is...
The 'fate' of Great Yan.

lines.loc: 1102 Late autumn

lines.x: Yo. Still working?
Madame Oni—I mean, Hoshiguma Sir...
Last Mid-Autumn Festival, Downtown was flooded with people watching the festival lanterns. Patrol didn't have enough hands to keep everyone off the roads; it almost got hairy out there.
The Commissioner was fuming over it...
Oho. Probably shouldn't have done that.
The whole squad got punished for it. Double patrol hours for the month.
The Commissioner even told us to be extra careful now, since Chief Wei has been out of town.
Sure. Better safe than sorry.
Well, good work, boys. I did just happen to bring in some chow, so share it amongst yourselves.
We can't possibly take all this...
C'mon, listen to your stomach growling. Have a bite.
Wait, what was that—
It came out of nowhere. What kind of thunderclap was that?
Wait, am I seeing things?
Over there, in the sky... what is that?
Boatman, slow us down. Look over there, do you see something on the river?
A beckbeast? But how is it on the river, and it's carrying... a goblet?
Just a mirage. What's so special about that, don't you get those in Kou-wu City?
(...Break.)
Huh, what's with the sudden rain...? Where'd the beckbeast go?
*sigh*...
Autumn rain this year... may well freeze my bones.
Reporting in! Report—
The Feranmut... has reached the final sentry post!
Red alert! Repeat, all units on maximum alert!
Wo kao...
That thing doesn't look like a 'creature' at all... that's a damned mountain...!
Excuse me, sir.
The Grand Commandant is sick abed, and will not see anyone, so please—
Is the Grand Commandant in?
Y-Yes!
Bring me to him.
*cough* *cough*... *cough* *cough* *cough*...
...In my fevered dreams, it was as if I'd seen the late...
I had thought... you were the late emperor, here to take me away.
I know you often think of the late emperor...
But I fear anxiety has addled your brain, if you're thinking about your end already.
Your Imperial Majesty... you honor me with your presence...
Forgive this servant... I've failed to rise for even a salutation...
How have you deteriorated so greatly, in the few days since our last meeting?
Are you taking your medicine?
I beg Your Majesty's understanding...
This consumption is a stubborn, chronic illness... The abnormal weather this Mid-Autumn struck with a cold...
I fear this wizened shell may not make it through the winter...
You are a trusted servant of Great Yan and should not sell yourself short.
How could the same man, who once kept countless foreign foes halted at our gates, dread a mere cold?
Be at ease, and convalesce. I await your swift recovery, and for you to share my burdens and aid the nation once you are well.
*cough* *cough*... This addled servant... is in truth, ill-suited to the task.
Great Yan today is rich with talent. Your Imperial Majesty decides all matters, great and small, so why seek counsel from this withered servant...?
...I see.
So the Grand Commandant is unsatisfied with my arbitrary actions, and wishes to return home due to his illness.
I... This humble servant would never dare...
In the last two weeks, ministers across the bureaucracy and all throughout the land have stacked petitions urging war high on my desk.
Every one is passionate, and a few I would venture to call desperate.
So for the Grand Commandant to claim illness at this hour, is this to compel me to...?
*cough* *cough*... Your Majesty is indeed aware...
All Yanese share a view on this age-old problem...
Your Majesty may not believe me, but ought to have faith in this...
The will of the people'.
I'll be willing to try any other possible solution, as long as we can avoid spilling the blood of our troops too readily.
I had thought you would know best my mind on the matter.
Your Majesty is well-versed in our history... *cough*—surely you must know...?
Across the many times the blood that ought be shed... when was it ever avoidable...?
One ought to act swiftly when necessary. Indecisive efforts for want of perfection will only lead to more bloodshed.
I had thought... Your Majesty understood this long ago...
It has been more than two weeks since the great offender entered the Tomb of Sui, but his fate remains unknown.
All Great Yan is in an uproar, not only due to the five Feranmuts, but the presence of foreign foes as well...
Leave him in the Tomb for even one more day, and the danger to Great Yan will increase tenfold.
This is not my opinion alone...
Even if I could temporarily suppress public sentiments for Your Majesty's sake... it would be no true solution...
So, how do you want to go about it?
I cannot ask Your Majesty to rescind an imperial edict...
I can only make an earnest request... to set a day.
...A final deadline until which Great Yan must hold.
Whether or not blood should have ever been shed will be determined thousands of years from now by historians and common folk alike.
But for now, I am afraid neither the Grand Commandant, nor I, will get to make that determination.
Is it Great Yan that cannot hold, or the collective lust for glory?
...Your Imperial Majesty...
But your request is not without merit.
I recall, it was the day after the full moon when he went in...
The next day of the full moon it is.
If the man fails to achieve anything in the Tomb of Sui, then we will proceed as the Grand Commandant proposes.
Your Imperial Majesty... is wise and just...
Let it be so.
A cold is no grave illness, Grand Commandant, but convalescence is still important.
I hear Danyan's snow pears are a great expectorant. I'll send for some.
Finish the pears before you rest for the night, and your illness should be cured in the morning.
I will hold court early tomorrow. Do not be late.

lines.loc: Three days before the day of the full moon

lines.x: Are we... outside?
Those Feranmut proxies... have quite the mystical prowess.
Is the Placation finally complete?
Perhaps...
No... just how many times have we tried? Is that complete?
How long have we spent in the Garden of Grotesqueries?
Based on the dates on the Tianshi Bureau's terminal... it's been more than two weeks since we entered the Garden.
More than half a month? Why do I feel as if we've spent even longer than that...?
We don't know if anything's happened in the Tomb of Sui since we've been here...
Zuo, you asked 'how many times'...?
I'm getting the sense this isn't our first time standing on this spot.
Concluding the Placation, taking the jade rule, leaving the Garden, and going back to town... I feel like we've done it all multiple times, and also...
Sometimes it's Mr. Yi who greets us, while other times the weiqi player blocks our path.
Sometimes the bestial Hou turns into a butterfly and gravely injures the bestial Sui... but other times we fall into a geist trap and get chased out of the Garden for no apparent reason.
And this hunch isn't a recent realization?
It was a faint feeling at first, but it was only just now that I suddenly realized we seem to have been repeating our Garden visits.
If that is the case, maybe the Placation is finally complete?
...I certainly hope that's the case.
Right, who has the token? It seems Mr. Yi hands the rule to a different person each time we complete the rite...
I don't have it.
Neither do I.
Then—
A small object falls from Zuo Le's pocket.
To everyone's surprise, it is no jade rule, but a tiny 'doll'.
What...?
Auntie Lin, Yun... Have we seen this before?
Yo, so you made it out alive. Pretty tough, huh?
Zushi!
Zu... Zushi.
Why are you calling me that when you're not even a Tianshi disciple, kiddo?
Um, how should I address you then...?
Who cares about titles? Your qinggong's good and you move quick, so just get to wherever you need to be.
Where do I need to be...?
What should a Candleholder do after getting the token from the Garden?
Return to the Sui Regulator and submit the jade rule along with a report.
Then begone! No more dawdling!
But the jade rule—
A token's a token. Maybe there's a reason you didn't get the jade rule this time, but just hurry back to the Sui Regulator already. Or are you scared that proxy's gonna report you for theft?
Qianbei, there's something I'd like to ask about Baizao—
Just be quick. Who's the Candleholder here, you or me?
Won't you know the answer once you see the city yourself anyway?
Act your age. I'm sure you know how to adapt to a situation, don't you?
...Yes.
I'll go with Zuo Le.
You stay.
Zushi...?
Baizao doesn't have much use for a judiciary at the moment, so it'd be a pointless trip for you. You'll be more useful here.
Same goes for the Yun kid. Neither of you is a member of Sui Regulator, so stay. You'll make less trouble for all of us.
The three exchange glances, and silently agree to the Tianshi's directive.
Zuo Le puts the token back into his pocket, and after bidding farewell to the party, leaps off northward.
Old... Qianbei, there's something I want to ask...
Where is the young lady who claimed to be of the Shanhaizhong, the one you took away before we entered the Tomb?
Oh? I didn't think a dimwit like you would concern yourself with that lass.
I just remembered, she mentioned there was something from the Garden that could keep Yan and the bestial Sui from coming to blows.
You granted us permission to enter the Garden, but you didn't examine the token Zuo Le had at all.
Does that mean... you believe she spoke the truth?
How intriguing... Smarty pants like you are so different from the martial types.
Of course, I believe you have your reasons for sending him alone, but I'm worried Zuo Le might come to harm if the token is truly important.
Hmph, to speak of safety... no one's truly safe right now.
If that kid really has what it takes to be a Candleholder, now's the time to show it.
What's wrong? I rarely ever see you so down.
I just worry they might not manage to deliver that token.
Those who know what the token truly is can only stand and watch, while those helping us don't even know where it should go.
That Old Tianshi has already done us a grand favor. The rest is up to the family's fate.
I didn't think those youths would get involved in this, and I'm not sure whether to be relieved or worried.
Many have come to this garden over the past century.
But this group of youngsters was the first to retrieve that glimmer of hope from our memories left behind and bring it back to this realm.
No matter what, we must believe that their fates are somewhat linked to ours.
You speak sense, sir.
Well, myriad sights abound in the land, but encounters are still up to fate.
You could buy a ticket early, do your research, memorize the map, stand outside the garden until it opens... but then a storm comes, and you see nothing at all.
Or you could enter through a random side gate and roam around aimlessly, but as long as you have a goal in mind, you might be pleasantly surprised by what you find.
Visitors come with interest, but it always wanes with time... It's not proper to force this enthusiasm to endure.
So let's just relax and wait here, and protect this garden...
This beautiful place is honestly quite difficult to abandon.
What a bother. I'm getting a sore throat repeating the same argument over and over.
We're back to square one, no? Thousands of years we've debated, and it seems nothing's getting through that thick head of yours.
You're the stubborn one. Whatever scholarly talents you have are negated by your expertise in nonsensical argumentation.
The truth is simple—there is no order without rules. To live in this realm, one must be able to tell right from wrong.
If it really is as you say, for everyone to blur boundaries and confuse right and wrong, what sort of chaos will befall this realm?
Preposterous... Your insistence on adherence to rules is nothing more than locking yourself in a prison of your own making.
Let me ask you then. To build a room, a house, a city, a moat—there are rules to follow, yes.
But when we measure merits and errors made in history, or the hearts of the masses—what rules or metrics are we to abide by?
Of course, rules are made to fit the situation, and for mortal benefit.
When have I ever said rules should be set for the sake of having them?
Where... are we?
...And who are you?
Fine, fine... then let me ask you this.
You say we must have rules, but then who should set them in the first place?
The virtuous, of course—only they can convince the masses, and rules can only stand when accepted by all.
Don't you see the problem with that?
We have to first find virtuous people to set rules, but what deems one virtuous? Doesn't it mean we have to lay down some rules to find these people?
Ju, the ten thousand years of your life have been a waste, if you can't even decide if the fowlbeast or egg came first.
Hah! You're just unreasonably putting the cart before the burdenbeast to suit your fallacy.
We already have rules called 'righteousness'. You think they just appeared from thin air?
What...?
What are you talking about...?
Oh, look. This one's about to wake up too.
Awake? Or dreaming?
Do you even have to ask? Just look at her, all lost. Obviously, she's dreaming.
Awake in a dream, yet not truly woken. The girl is suffering from a headache over the dilemma she faces.
If you're stubborn and refuse to see sense, you'll surely run up against barriers, both physical and mental.
You're right about that... her knowing and doing don't match. She's contradicting herself—wants non-action, yet also wants to act.
Now, she seeks a tangible form of 'justice', and even wants to pocket it and take it away. I think she's far more obsessed than that good-for-nothing disciple of mine.
Yes, how could she enjoy such a delusional dream if she weren't the most obsessed person walking this realm?
No need to be so harsh, Ju. Plenty of people out there climb trees to catch fins or strike ice to make fire. What can we even do?
But you're right about one thing...
Is this girl an obsessed one, or what...?
Who—
What's the matter with you?
Nothing...
Did you not hear any of that?
You're so weird. You've been staring into blank space for a while now.
Did those geists give you a concussion? Don't suppose there's anything I can do about that.
Thanks. I'm not quite—
I just feel like...
I've been here before...

lines.st: 'Hui-chieh... your name is Ch'en Hui-chieh.'
'Remember this family name. The rest has nothing to do with you.'
'Tal... is my sister?'
'I hate you. And I hate them. I should love you, but right now I hate every last one of you.'
'Hui-chieh, grow up soon.'
'When you grow up... you'll change everything.'

lines.loc: Three days before the day of the full moon

lines.x: ...Thunder.
And nothing more.
It's late autumn. If you wait till winter, the roads will be hard to traverse.
...Hmph.
Hui-chieh... you should go back.
'Go back'?
To where? Lungmen?
I've already told you what you want to know.
So you should understand, all that lies in front of you is none of your business.
Hah... I think you've gotten old, Uncle.
If you were five years younger, we would have crossed swords already.
Your temperament has changed too.
You don’t have many issues here, over the past few days at this posthouse.
Because I still don't trust you.
What you said... about the Grand Preceptor, about your brother—that 'other uncle' I've never met, and the old True Lung...
It's not my place to comment, but if I have to say something, I'm just glad you're not the one running Yan.
I cannot argue with that...
I really do want to know what was going on in your head when you left Baizao, why Mother followed you to Lungmen, and why she stayed.
But at this point, I'm not expecting to hear a fair answer—or an objective account of the past from you.
So I'll find the answers myself—and I'm not leaving until I do.
You have a knack for messing things up when it matters most, especially when it's about you.
To stop you from doing anything stupid again, or even just to keep you safe—I'm not leaving until I'm done.
So let's both save our breath.
Hui-chieh... these past few years, you—
Same as always. Nothing worth sharing.
Perhaps it's better to wait until you're willing...
Ch'en Hui-chieh looks out the window.
The lights of the far-off nomadic city have been dim for days, leaving Baizao's skyline vague at night, like the spine of a Feranmut in hiding.
She tries to imagine the colossal creatures from ancient fables only recently confirmed as real to her.
Fine autumn rain drizzles against the window, like pins and needles on glass.
Still no news.
Then that's the best news we could ask for.
There is no hoping for a positive turn of events, at this point.
So, if anything strange happens in Baizao... Yumen won't hesitate to go to war.
This 'bestial Sui' you talk of, the unrest it might cause, and any battle it might provoke... no one's sure how any of it could turn out.
Correct.
So this is the secret Great Yan has hidden for over a thousand years... You two are so alike.
There is a strange detail, though.
Time is running out, but in the past ten days, there have been no more than three Messenger parties traveling between Yumen and Baizao.
When Yumen is facing war, we would expect far more traffic.
Lord Wei, a visitor.
At a time like this...?
Send them in.
I trust you've been well, Lord Wei.
Likewise, Grand Tutor.
So you're... the Grand Tutor of Yan?
I did not know Lady Ch'en would be here as well.
How do you...?
Ch'en is certain she has never met this old man before.
She cannot tell if it is an austere or kind look in his eyes, but he recognizes her instantly.
Swiftly, he turns that same gaze to Wei Yenwu.
That's another matter, and a long story.
I see.
So you did heed my advice to stay at this posthouse.
I have no grounds to distrust the Grand Tutor.
It has been Messengers, but today the Grand Tutor graces us with a personal visit...
There is something I must make clear to you, Lord Wei.
Hui-chieh, leave us.
No need.
Best she stay and hear what I am about to say.
Fine.
The situation in Baizao...
After Mid-Autumn, His Imperial Majesty granted him leave to enter the Tomb of Sui, to try and quell the threat posed by the bestial one.
But the day of the full moon nears, and we've heard nothing from the tomb.
And yet, at this very moment, five Feranmuts have lurked around Great Yan, with intentions unknown.
The Tianshis of the Chamber of Heaven's Designs along with Yan's finest warriors have stationed themselves near the Feranmuts. Great Yan does not fear the coming battle.
But the worst outcome we face, is not just fighting five Feranmuts simultaneously.
No way...
'Intentions unknown'—This gray beard sure can talk.
With the blood feud of the past few millennia, did you think they'd simply show up on your doorstep bearing gifts?
Huh...?
What... did you say?
I'm sure many are advocating for war at this time.
There is no opinion from any party left to consider.
I fear Great Yan is left with no other choice.
The court must have drafted plenty of plans for this contingency.
Countless plans, and none end without a pyrrhic victory.
But there is still a method yet to be considered.
Tell me, Grand Tutor. Tell me my brother has never conceived of activating Bu Fan himself—
He is the True Lung. The True Lung of Yan.
If the nation is driven to a corner, he is the only one who can do it.
It's absurd!
Lord Wei.
As it stands, there is no longer a need to digress.
Not a single man knew of the secret letter sent to Lungmen until the Imperial Guards set off. And I come today without any attempt to conceal my identity.
I'm sure you understand, Lord Wei.
The past... stories from forty years ago, thousands of years ago... it should not end like this.
...And he should never be the one to pay the price.
No one wants to see the worst-case scenario, but we cannot deceive ourselves.
I must remind you—even if merely for Yan's sake, or for the good of the realm.
A backup plan is called for.
Please, just slow down a second.
I don't get it. What does the Grand Tutor mean by a backup plan?
Everything you're saying... what does any of it have to do with Wei Yenwu?
You still don't get it?
You don't know what a backup plan is? Something you do when things go south.
What... are you talking about?
A kingdom cannot be without its king for even a day. A missing True Lung must naturally see his seat filled by another.
When father dies, son takes over, and when elder brother leaves, younger returns. That's how it's been for thousands of years.
And if there's no other choice, this lass will have no choice but to become the True Lung.
What the hell are you talking about?!
...Hui-chieh!
That voice...
Did the two of you... not hear any of that?
Lord Wei.
I do not expect you to have considered all possible angles already.
One must think before one acts, but time is short.
Take care, Lord Wei.
I'll see you out.
No need.
I didn't get any of what he was saying.
He had me stay to hear it, but I couldn't make sense of it at all.
Hui-chieh...
All that stuff about Yan probably going to war, but he'll give you time to prepare?
Bu Fan, the Feranmuts... what does any of it have to do with you?
Hui-chieh.
Many mistakes from the past... Some I made myself, but others I was powerless to stop.
I only insist on ensuring those irrevocable mistakes do not affect the rest of you.
Because I've never doubted in your potential to accomplish more than we ever could.
This is the worst time you could be saying that.
One more thing.
Talulah...
Tell me. Was she well, the last time you met?
No way. I'm not telling you about her right now.
If you really care, then now is not the time.
Aunt Fumizuki doesn't want you to stay a stubborn old man forever, and she's worried about your safety. And I don't want to—
If you truly care for anyone but yourself, then don't leave them behind.
Tell me, Uncle...
What exactly are you going to do?
It sounds like she's doing fine. Well, not bad, at least.
That's not your call to make!
You don't know how much she's sacrificed, and you've no clue what she's—
Enough...
If you want to enjoy your pity party here, then have fun.
I'm going out for some fresh air.
Fine autumn rain continues unabated, like intermittent thoughts left tangled with no end.
I’m sick of you, Wei Yenwu.
You and your precious city.
Where are you headed?
Back up north.
There are people in the mines who need our help.
We're running out of time.
Right now?
Then what about this farm? What about the people left here?
Some of our comrades are staying. They'll take care of each other.
You could join them too, if you're so inclined.
I can't just let you go...
You could follow us instead.
I'd like your help, if it's on the table.
But—
Hui-chieh.
I believe in your words, and your ideals... a place where the Infected are judged fairly... and can live, truly live.
But that place is not here—not in the past, not in the present.
The place we seek lies only in the future.
And must make our way there.
Alright, so on we go... and what next?
Did my past catch up with me... or did I go looking for it? Heh, guess it's less history and more a reality I haven't faced yet.
Talulah, if you were here... what would you think about all these?
Hmph. Overcautious and indecisive. Your courage is for naught.
This lass is unlike her ancestors.
That first Lung's descendants must number at least tens of thousands over the millennia, how many of them were heroes, and how many were mediocrities?
How much can this sliver of that bloodline even accomplish?
With a mind this turbid, as if trapped in a dream, can we really trust her with anything?
Or is it because those old blockheads refuse to bend? No wonder the youths are too inept to handle things on their own.
She's been on quite the journey, so she must have other special skills beyond her brute swordplay.
Just who the hell are you?!
Enough hiding!
Come out here and show yourselves!
The vague, mysterious voice speaks again, but she hears it clearly this time.
Only now is Ch'en Hui-chieh sure the whispers that have plagued her all night are no mere hallucination.
Hah, just look at the girl. Inexperienced, yet quick to anger.
I'm glad you want to know, but if you truly seek your answer, why not find it yourself—
What the...?
What was that?
From the posthouse?
Lady Ch'en.
What are you all standing around for... did something happen?
When was the last time you saw Lord Wei?
Barely an hour ago.
So you don't know his whereabouts either.
Wait, you mean—
Half an hour ago, Lord Wei went to rest and dismissed us.
The posthouse owner just realized the room was empty when delivering the food.
We've searched the premises, and found no trace.
Lord Wei is... missing.
No note left in the room.
An empty room, as if it never housed a guest.
So he actually managed to slip out, with all of you watching.
Again... yet again, Wei Yenwu.
Lord Wei received a letter, personally penned by the True Lung, summoning him to the capital. We tried in vain to dissuade him. We could only follow.
Let us tentatively assume he is making his way there.
We failed to ensure his safety, and our crime is unforgivable. However, I fear entering the capital now would only—
Don't worry.
Ch'en interrupts the Shadow Guard.
Whatever the case, he left without a word.
So this time... I'll find him.

lines.loc: Two days before the day of the full moon, Hour of Yin

lines.x: Reporting in—from the Hour of Yu yesterday to the Hour of Yin today, we have moved more than eighty percent of the residents from the area around the main street to nomadic plate C.
Some residents remain due to official duties, property concerns, or other reasons.
Urge them to settle their affairs at once and leave material goods behind. Time is almost up.
Understood!
Also, strengthen security inspections at checkpoints. Report any suspicious individuals or situations to your captain immediately.
Yes, sir!
So Baizao's already evacuating the population... I wonder if the Sui Regulator has any news from the Tomb of Sui.
That's a lot of sentries... best not draw attention to myself.
Time for a detour.
That's—
Zuo Le goes around the checkpoint, but as he turns a corner, he spots someone who should not be there.
Zuo Le.
Are you...?
Apologies, but may I ask... who you are, sir?
The Sui Regulator is aware of your deeds at Mangshan Town and in the Garden of Grotesqueries. I was dispatched to meet you here. You've done well.
You must be exhausted. Go to rest.
Thank you for your concern.
But I have to deliver a token to the Sui Regulator, and share the details of the Placation with the Supervisor at once, so I dare not retire for now.
Just the proof of the Placation. Fret not, just hand it here and I'll take care of it.
As for the report, you may submit it at a later time.
But...
Zuo Le subconsciously rubs the token in his pocket with his knuckles.
This is a special case, so I must submit the token and my report to the Supervisor myself.
Still doubting me, when I have the approval penned personally by the Supervisor for my actions right here?
Zuo Le, I know you are very conscientious, but the situation is complicated. You need not be stubborn.
I'm sorry, sir... but this is all too sudden, and I don't know where this change is coming from.
I do not doubt the Supervisor's orders, but could we at least head over to the Sui Regulator together so I can get a complete grasp of the situation?
Sir... what is the meaning of this?
Is someone obstructing official duties here?
Stay your hand... for now.
I've never seen the Sui Regulator cooperate with the Patrol Battalion before.
As I said, things are complicated. This is how affairs are handled in wartime.
Zuo Le, tarry any longer and we may hinder our battle plans.
I'm acting on orders. If you have any further questions, save them for the Supervisor herself later.
'Acting on orders'... dare I ask whose orders these are?
That is classified military information that may not be leaked, Lord Zuo. I'm sure you understand, seeing as you grew up in the barracks.
Three hundred Candleholders in the Sui Regulator, and none know each other's past.
So, how do you know my background?
Just who are you?
Seize him.
Go!
What—?
A black sphere suddenly rolls down to the center of their stalemate, spewing out thick black smoke that spreads quickly through the air.
An ambush! Assume defensive positions!
Just run already!
Wha—
Ah!
Zuo Le jumps up onto the eaves, mid-exclamation.
After him!
Footsteps rumble behind the young man as several soldiers rush in his direction.
Form a line! There's still time, no need to rush!
Think carefully. Did you leave it at your desk?
Y-Yeah! Probably!
What's the matter?
Sir!
We finished an experiment outside the city, and only just the evacuation order...
But we left something important in the school laboratory. We'll be back once we've fetched it, I promise! Can we go?
Time isn't on our side, what could be so important...?
It's really important data! We spent years just to collect it!
If we can iron out the issue, then maybe... just maybe...
Show me your student card, and then go over there for a security inspection.
Thank you, sir!
Right, once you get the data, evacuate with the Academy's staff. Make sure you leave within twenty-four hours.
Of course!
Hold it there, girl!
Me?
Young lady, Baizao is undergoing a mass evacuation. There is no entrance to the city without special permission.
May I ask what brings you here?
Ch'en tries to blend in with those entering the city, but the keen-eyed guard spots her immediately, and approaches.
She makes sure Chi Xiao stays unseen behind her.
I'm... looking for my uncle.
He's old, has trouble walking, and brought a lot of luggage on this trip—so my aunt asked me to escort him out.
Hm? But when the Ministry of Revenue issued the evacuation order two days ago, they brought out every elderly resident.
My uncle isn't a local... He's a tourist from Lungmen, who came here for Mid-Autumn.
He's... kind of a loner, and rarely talks to people. He's spent the last two days waiting for me to come pick him up at the inn.
Your papers, please...
...Sure.
Lungmenite... Ch'en Hui-chieh... former Senior Superintendent of the L.G.D.
And you're... an Infected?
I retired from the force because of it, but that was so long ago...
Thank you for your service. You may go.
If you don't manage to find your uncle, seek the nearest guard for help.
But make sure you leave the city within twenty-four hours.
Thanks for the heads up.
Right, could I just ask... Have you seen a tall Lung with red hair and horns pass through here since yesterday?
No...
But the description is somewhat familiar...
Don't worry about it. Just a passing thought.
Duoxie. I'll be going now.
Wait, miss! Where are you headed—
A muster signal?!
What's happening over there?
What...?
Retreat! Everyone, run! Take cover!
Attention all units—ensure civilians inside the sentry posts are safe!
O-Over there... what's that black mass?
In the dark of night, as thick clouds shroud the moonlight's glow, an 'army' descends upon the streets.
No commands, no war drums, no rattling of weapons against armor—even their approach makes no sound.
A disciplined, formidable, but still indistinct battalion closes in on the checkpoint with a deathly silence.
Hey... did you hear about the ghosts in Baizao... before the city moved onto a nomadic plate...?
A ghost army...
You believe in folk tales too? Probably a mirage caused by the strange weather lately!
It's way too realistic to be a mirage...
No, look at their armor! It's totally different from ours!
That's no mirage—

The army closes in, and the guard finally gets a clear look at the moving mass. Silent soldiers bear blurry, yet terrifying faces with old, bloodstained armor, like evil spirits from thousands of years ago.
A soldier's spear point nears the guard's neck before he can even react.
Fall back!
A shadow?
The Imperial Guard apprentice slashes forward, but merely changes the spear's trajectory, while the immense strength hidden in the soldier's attack startles her.
The spear point sweeps above her head, but the weapon unnaturally whips backwards, heading for her vitals.
There's nothing inside—
The soldier is destroyed by her slash.
Strangely, the other soldiers do not treat her as a foe, and simply advance along the empty road silently with no discernible destination.
Captain!
Send word to the other barracks: these soldiers are anomalous enemies; exercise extreme caution against them.
They don't seem to be actively hostile, so don't initiate conflict.
Evacuate anyone along the way and be on guard for developments.
Captain, where did they come from? What even are they...?
...Geists.
Dispatch some men to update the Sui Regulator at once.
They're merely marching forward for now, but we cannot allow them to endanger civilians or trespass into restricted areas.
At once!
I fear this unrest indicates whatever's happening inside the Tomb of Sui must be quelled immediately.
Could His Majesty's decree actually be...
Are they... already fighting?
A crimson sword flash?
My goodness... where did this army come from?!
Th-They're headed our way! Help!
'Scriptgeists'....
Still the same old look. Whose ancient dwelling dream is this?
Is it because It woke up? Or because you've returned home?
Look out!
Are you alright, madam—
Thank you.
How can I ever repay you?
What? No, there's no need for that...
I don't know where these creatures crawled out of, but they're dangerous. Just be careful.
Ch'en notices the woman calmly ignoring the surrounding commotion. She even turns to look at Ch'en herself.
Um...
Noticing the woman's scrutinizing gaze, Ch'en immediately sheathes Chi Xiao—in vain.
A descendant of the House of Yan.
You must have misunderstood, I—
What say you—what's the difference between forcing one person's will upon the masses, and enlightening the masses with the insight of one mind?
Where's this question coming from?
You do not have an answer now?
This isn't the best time to discuss it.
What a shame.
Perhaps we can continue our conversation another time.
...Perhaps.
The woman does not wait for Ch'en to respond before melting back into the crowd.
Strange lady.
Actually, are these monsters that popped up out of nowhere... related to the 'bestial Sui'?
They seem to be coming from the same direction—
The Patrol Battalion flashes across his view as Zuo Le hides behind a wall.
What's going on inside the city...? Whose orders are they following? And why are they so fixated on this token from the Garden?
That approval letter was indeed written in the Supervisor's own hand... but why would she be working with them—
More troops?!
No, that's... that's?!
Countless paintings flash through his mind, until it stops on a scroll depicting the Great Hunt in the Sui Regulator's hidden collection.
I recall a similar scroll in Father's study.
Could they be... geists?!
As he stays deep in thought, the leading geist suddenly rushes towards him.
Look out!
The black geist disperses, leaving splotches of black ink on the nearby walls.
(This inkstain... a scriptgeist?!)
(But how? Don't tell me, that vanished proxy...)
My Lord! Are you—
Pester me any longer and I shan't show you mercy!
Th-There's a misunderstanding, sir!
I saw you getting attacked by that monster, so I ran here to help...
You're not apprehending me?
Huh?
My Lord must be joking... I only just received orders to assist the Candleholders in cleansing the monsters...
Have you seen any other Candleholders?
N-No.
(The city is in turmoil, but the Sui Regulator hasn't sent anyone...)
(And the Patrol Battalion... obviously two different directives have been issued...)
(I must get to the bottom of this, once I meet with the Supervisor.)
My apologies... this was my mistake. Please excuse me.
It's dangerous here. Keep safe.
His sword flashes in a spin as Zuo Le dispatches several nearby geists, and he instantly vanishes into an alley.
S-So fast!
Gah... why the rush?
More and more scriptgeists this way...
Where exactly did they come from...?
—Come out!
Alright, alright... Here I am, don't get mad now.
It's you—!
You, girl, you're coming with me.
You don't get along with that Candleholder boy, and you'll just keep fighting in there. Whatever you're looking for, he can find it for you.
And what is your intention, having followed me the whole way?
Why the attitude? Shouldn't you be happy, reuniting with an acquaintance?
You...
I believe the Old Tianshi has her reasons for letting you go.
But I can't trust you... a Shanhaizhong.
Fine, fine... O righteous and benevolent Lord Zuo of the Sui Regulator, I know you look down upon us lot.
But you're being hunted down by your own people. Surely we can cooperate a little?
I believe there's been a misunderstanding... I'll go to see the Supervisor, and from there, I will naturally—
Hold on, how do you know the Sui Regulator is after me?
You numbskull! They would be walking you back right now, if I hadn't stepped in when they were surrounding you back there!
Wait, that was you—
Duh! Who else would dare get in the Sui Regulator's way?
I saved your life, so could you be just a bit nicer, maybe?
There are geists in the vicinity, miss—
So you should be careful.
Damn it... these blasted things are crawling everywhere. Situation's even worse than I thought.
Enough nonsense. Where's the thing you got from the Garden?
What...? You want it too?
I'm not telling you.

Hey—
Huff...!
She turns around, pointing towards the growing mass of scriptgeists, accumulating from all directions.
Ignore me, and you'd better have a way to deal with all of that.
Huh?
You know how to use that token?
I... do not.
Then just zip it and hand it over!
After a momentary reluctance, Zuo Le finally takes out the 'doll' he took so much care to keep on himself.
This color... it really is her.
Where did you get it?
In a study, or was it a memorial hall?
I can't recall... I don't know why, but our memories of the Garden are a mess.
I have a question, though. Is this object, as you say, able to stop Yan and the bestial Sui from going to war?
I'll tell you later. For now, we're going to use it to take care of these ugly things!
Mo Yi unceremoniously snatches the token from him.
Hey—!
All to break the stalemate—the only way out.
The girl leaps forward, putting distance between her and Zuo Le.
(—The forbidden Arts from that day!)
Wait, you can't do that inside the city—
With ink flowing as years roll on, annals are wrought; through spring and autumn's turns, the tale of countless lives is told—
In the name of the Shanhaizhong, I beseech thee: Fifth of the Twelve, bearing the name Jie—manifest!
She holds the token with a stern expression, and recites her chant—
But it seems... as if nothing is happening?
Huh... so that's not how you use it.
My guess was off...?
What?!
Oh well, works for me.
Let me borrow this for a bit. I'll give it back once I'm done studying it.
Wait! You're not going anywhere!

lines.loc: Two days before the day of the full moon, Hour of Ch'en

lines.x: The geists vanished with the sunrise.
They were all coming from here. So this must be the source.
The Sky Mirror Pavilion... this is the place?
Who is it?
Stop right there!

lines.st: Here you are.
The same game of weiqi... one that you have lost countless times over the centuries.
You are trying to fight the whole with its parts... all well-laid plans no more than child's play in my eyes.
Drowning yourself in children's toys. A bad old habit of yours.
This challenge is simply a repetition of the thousands of defeats of the past.

lines.x: ...Hah.
You try to mask your fear with arrogance.
Why bother?
Anger, fear, hope... I see clearly in you the things you try to see in me.
Join the game, if you're not afraid.
This time...
You WILL lose.
I am defeated.

lines.loc: Year 593

lines.x: I did not expect this variation of the offset play.
You had a chance, if you did not continue to cede ground in the center around the hundredth move, hoping for a draw.
I did not have a clear view of the developments to come, so I picked the safe move. I see I was mistaken.
Haha. I thought my weiqi skills had improved, but it looks like I'm still far beneath you.
In this last battle, we were only able to avoid heavy casualties thanks to your strategy of conserving our strength and luring the enemy deep in.
This return to the capital is an opportunity for you to rest. But I expect it will be a long time until our next meeting.
You don't seem concerned with victory or defeat.
Why obsess over such things in a meeting between brothers?
Not just weiqi. It seems that nothing ever concerns you; you are always content to act the part of the kind benefactor.
I don't know why you'd say that...
The new Right Assistant Minister of the Ministry of War, together with officials from the Ministry of Rites, accused you in a petition to the throne two months ago.
Commanding troops to act without authorization. A serious crime.
How do you know the imperial court's business, when you've been with me on the front lines for the past six months?
I have my ways. You should worry about yourself.
If he meant our late return to camp, it was because we had to stop and wait for a Catastrophe to pass.
General Yang knew. The Ministry of War hardly has a case.
Oh, you don't need to worry about that.
The Assistant Minister was found to have accepted bribes and embezzled supplies... he is in prison now, awaiting trial.
He won't spread rumors any longer for now. As for whether he makes it out of prison alive... only Heaven knows.
You promised to stop meddling in the affairs of the court.
I would hardly call it meddling. Simply helping Yan rid itself of a corrupt official.
You know your own intentions...
You see the entire court as a game of weiqi, with human lives for stones?
Has your lust for honesty and righteousness blinded you to the evil and malice lurking in the hearts of people?
No sooner had that man taken office than he started to target you. Do you really think there is no one behind the curtain? Do you really believe we have only one enemy?
Go easy on this one, and we set ourselves up for more trouble to come.
Strange things have been happening since the birth of our youngest brother.
Fāng's patient committed suicide after getting better.
Someone has been producing 'satirical' works of falsehood in Dusk's name, imitating her touch.
Thieves have stolen treasures from Yi's garden. A long and thorough investigation by the Judicial Review Office turned up nothing more than a gang of fools.
And then, your matter.
We all know... It is not dead.
The people here have never forgotten Its terror. That is why they watch our every move, every meeting.
Do you still think your little achievements will force them to see us as anything more than alien entities, enemies to be feared?
Are Fāng and Dusk alright?
Fāng was depressed for a while. Dusk has always been how she is. But I don't suppose either would worry too much about it.
But what about the future?
You think someone is pulling the strings behind the scenes?
The Shanhaizhong, or someone else with ulterior motives?
That's what I truly fear.
If a single mastermind is behind all of this...
I cannot determine who it might be, nor do I have any inkling of their intentions.
I will confirm all that you've said with the Sui Regulator.
...I know you have good intentions.
But don't overstep the boundaries like this again.
Since when have we been worried for one another?
I simply didn't want to fall into a trap without knowing who dug it.
It's time to go.
The journey is far. Be careful on your way.
Bring the others my greetings.
...I may not see them.
*sigh*.
The proxy bids his sibling farewell and proceeds in the direction of the capital.
The journey is far, and he is in a hurry. He does not have time to stop for small noises along the way.
One wrong move...
And all is lost...
Let's begin with the name 'Yu', shall we?
Sure. Let me think...
Yu Yin Rao Liang.' The sound lingers upon the beams.
Liang. Liang...
...What idiom begins with 'Liang'?
Don't tell me you're giving up already.
O-Of course not. Just busy with the beastmeat. Give me some time.
Sure... I have many books to copy. Take your time.

Liang... I feel like I should ask Yi, but he's not here...
Heng Liang'—crossbeam. 'Fang Liang'—roof beam. 'Dong Liang'—pillar. I know! 'Dong Liang Zhi Cai,' pillar of society!
Wait, it has to start with Liang...
(Say, she has so many bamboo slips with words on them sitting next to her. Maybe if I took a peek...)
What is it?
—N-Nothing.
Why are you copying the words from the slips onto these... squares?
It's called 'paper'. A new invention.
It's lighter and easier to write on than bamboo. It also doesn't deteriorate easily, which makes it easy to preserve.
You can preserve something forever, if you write it on paper?
Yes and no.
Bamboo slips may last for decades, paper for centuries.
Be it bamboo or paper, however, there ultimately comes a day when it perishes.
No matter what we use to record something, it will only endure forever if people truly commit it to memory.
Just like how people remember your cooking when they taste it.
Hm...
He listens, but it is unclear if he understands. He scratches his head beside the stockbeast that he has just butchered, but he cannot come up with an idiom that starts with 'Liang'.
Suddenly, a small piece of bamboo falls in front of him, and he sees the words on it.
Liang Shang Jun Zi'—man on the roof beam!
Not bad. 'Zi', eh? Let me think... 'Zi Xu—'
Let's stop here. This word chain game is so hard. Why don't you play it with Ling and Dusk the next time they're here, and I'll watch from the sidelines.
Are you hungry? Do you want me to cook the meat?
Sure.
Can I have the bamboo slips, if you don't need them anymore?
For the fire? I suppose...
The young proxy takes the bamboo slips from his sister and skillfully cuts them down the middle...
Into long, sharp sticks.
What are you making?
Meat skewers!
This stockbeast tenderloin is just the right mix of lean and fat, and the fat on its belly is nice and firm. It'll taste great roasted.
The fuel has to be charcoal. These bamboo slips don't burn for long.
I already have the charcoal. We can start roasting when I'm done cutting the slips and skewering the meat.
Hah... does grilling meat on bamboo slips make this a scholar's meal?
Let's call it 'scholar's barbecue'!
Well, I trust your cooking. I'm excited to try it.
Well, I'm done with the slips. You can take them.
You're both here.
Oh... back already?
Huh...?
Wang?
I got your letter. I thought you wouldn't be here for a few more days, at least.
Smooth trip?
Yes.
You two...
There's the idiom 'burning a guqin to cook a fowlbeast,' which means sacrilegious, wasteful behavior.
What are you doing with these bamboo slips?
I-I'm not...
Jie gave them to me...
The boy hides behind his sister, peeking his head out from her side.
Just giving some broken bamboo slips to Yu to make the most of them. Why so harsh?
You should be more grateful to Yu for making dinner.
It's not like I actually said anything...
Don't fight, don't fight!
I'll go make dinner! It's not every day my second-oldest brother comes back. We finally get to eat together!
You, just, er, sit down and chat!
*sigh*...
He's always been a little afraid of you. Can't you be more gentle?
Are things not going well?
No.
I see...
But we don't get together very often, and Yu misses you.
Anything you want to say can wait until after dinner.
So, unsettling things have been happening without my knowledge.
You don't look worried at all.
I'm worried... and I'm just surprised that you show so much concern for your siblings, brother.
Siblings in the sense that we were born from the same fragmented consciousness. Not true blood relations.
We each have our own interests and ambitions. Our fates are none of each other's concern... we walk different paths.
Do you truly think so?
My views are different.
See, it's been centuries since we came to the mortal world. Even if we are not truly part of it, most of the scenes and people that we see are lovely.
But the people we meet seem to become smoke in the wind in the blink of an eye.
In the end, it is only 'us' who remain, and it's only in each other that we can truly speak our hearts.
We may have differences and we may have conflicts, but we could never cut each other off completely... isn't that very much like human siblings?
I have no intention of arguing, if this is how you think.
*sigh*... I don't know why you always seem so aloof. We don't see each other very often. Are you not happy at all?
I wonder if you'd regret it, if one of us suddenly disappeared forever.
Speaking of which, I encountered something strange recently.
One night, I was about to go to bed when I heard a sound from the study.
From the garden, I saw through the window the silhouette of someone bending over the desk, writing something.
I hurried into the study to find no one, only the Jijiuzhang that I had been transcribing the past few days lying on the desk, with scribblings all over it...
Jie takes a scroll from the desk.
One word is written over every neatly written character on the page.

lines.st: Sui

lines.x: Who do you think could have done it?
There is only one possibility.
It is awakening, and It is attempting to interfere with us in some way.
We thought Its consciousness had completely faded after Yu's birth.
But It is alive... still sleeping, but the stuff of our nightmares.
If It does wake, we will become Its fleeting dream...
What do you have in mind?
I don't know yet. But I'll think of a way...
I have an idea, if you'd care to listen.
I only recently thought about it... when I compiled the histories with the people of Yan, there were inevitably some mistakes, no matter how careful we were.
Unlike ordinary writing, it takes great effort to correct mistakes written in the histories.
Jie picks up a writing knife from the desk, used to scrape bamboo slips, then picks up a scroll, and gently scrapes the paper.

The ink that has seeped into the paper, even smearing the next page slightly, disappears without a trace when the writing knife touches it. The paper is blank, as though nothing has ever been written on it.
This knife...
I think my influence has changed it.
I have a faint feeling that the words it scrapes away—a history, or at least... a name—don't just disappear from the paper; they disappear from people's hearts and minds.
Perhaps there will come a day when we use it to scrape the word 'Sui' from all the texts of this world.
You can... do that?
Not yet. Right now, it's only a writing knife, albeit an unusual one.
But I can feel it. The more words written, the heavier my knife and pen become.

Perhaps we're all the same. We discovered the answer to who we are in that primordial void, then we understood the meaning of our existence in the mortal realm. This is what proves we are different from It.
Perhaps there will come a day when our strength exceeds Its. Then, we can truly break free of Its influence, and live as 'ourselves'.
As long as we don't forget who we are, of course.
What are you thinking on, brother?
Don't worry about it.
*sigh*... Sometimes I feel like you and I are the least compatible, amongst our siblings.
Plots and plans are things to be kept inside one's heart, but words and language are for expressing what lies in one's heart.
But sometimes I feel like you hide nothing from me, that I can see all that you think.
Interesting, isn't it? We both found what we wanted to devote ourselves to, only to have it reshape our personalities in return.
Shu always says I'm obsessed with the past. And I remember the deathly white that she talked about, which lies at the end of the future.
She was right. One cannot pretend to be carefree, when there are things that one cannot let go of.
I know you're worried about the unknowable future... victory and defeat are what you're most obsessed about, but it would be such a miserable existence, if there was nothing but victory and defeat.
Besides that, what is it you care about the most?
I do not obsess about victory and defeat. I simply...
I simply do not like to accept that a game could not be won.
Hm...
By the way, are you reporting to the Sui Regulator tomorrow? Could you take these copied books with you? They'll need to review them before they can be archived in the Sky Mirror Pavilion.
Very well.
And take another look at the re-copied Jijiuzhang for me. Bring the writing knife too, so you can correct any mistakes.
Anything you've written hardly needs me to check it.
Consider it an activity to pass the time.
You spend so much time devising military strategies, thinking of victory and defeat. A distraction would do you good.
And perhaps, you might find what you're looking for in the text.

lines.loc: Year 593

lines.st: Better to sacrifice a few stones... than to lose the initiative...
One wrong move... and all is lost!
And all is lost...
Lost...

lines.x: ...So you found me.
You've been following me for quite a while. Show yourself.
Wang looks back. The mixture of weeping and lament moves away gradually, but it does not stop for even an instant.
Hmph.
Let me see what you've got.
Wang follows the eerie voice to a decrepit weiqi stall.
A man, presumably the owner of the stall, sits on the ground weeping. There is no one around.
One wrong move... and all is lost...
And all is lost!
Before Wang has a chance to take in the situation on the weiqi board, the man falls upon it and begins to wail, sending stones flying.
Ridiculous.
As Wang turns around to leave, the man's crying begins to abate.
Don't go, sir, please, I'll set up the board again...
This game cost me my life savings, my home, my farm, even my family scattered and some of them died because of it... I am very sick and don't have long to live.
A game. A game of weiqi, that cost me my home, my family, my life...
I couldn't let it go. Take a look for me. If you can find a solution...
...Perhaps fate can still be changed!
The man goes from sobbing to howling. As Wang stands motionless, the man picks up the stones from the ground, and quickly sets up the board.
Setup, probe, entrap, sacrifice, fight, exchange...

The man's hands move at a breathtaking pace. The game begins to grow as though it is alive, the war raging from the corners to the center, then to the other side which still lies empty, so fast the eyes can hardly keep up.
Before Wang can react, the man has put a black stone in his hand.
Your turn, sir.
...How?
Yes. How, indeed?
Black and white should have taken turns placing stones. But no black stone can be seen on the board, once you hold one. How should the game proceed?
This is ridiculous... I'll take my leave.
You ought to understand, sir.
This board is like an abyss. Every white stone is the size of a mountain, while the black ones that I have presumptuously placed upon it... are no more than game pieces.
We are so far from the board that even the mountains are no greater than the size of a thumb. How could we possibly see a tiny weiqi stone?
I held on as long as I could, but at this point I was dazed and exhausted. One wrong move...
The man begins to weep bitterly again—
...And all is lost!
Look! I gave each stone a name, but I couldn't save any of them! There is no way out for any of them!
This one is Shuo. This one is Wang. This one is Ling. This one is Jun—
Wang draws his blade and stabs at one of the white stones, only for it to make a sharp cracking sound as it bends, as though he were stabbing a boulder.
Pointless, like striking a rock with an egg...
Why did I play this game? I shouldn't have. Why...?
The sword turns and pierces the man's throat. He slumps to the ground, but no blood flows from the wound. The weeping grows even louder, though it is no longer intelligible.
The man's eyes glow with a strange expression. Is it fear or joy? He stares straight at Wang's face.
Wang instinctively reaches for the writing knife on his belt, but stops mid-way.
Finally, he ponders for a moment and puts the black stone in his hand on the board.
All is lost? Nonsense.
Set it here, and the game remains a toss-up.
No sooner have the words left Wang's mouth than the body on the ground turns into smoke, and the invisible black stones on the board reveal themselves.
Mr. Wang!
You...
I watched you stand in front of the door to the Sui Regulator before turning around again. I came to find you as I thought you might have met with a strange happening too.
Strange happening? You already know?
Yes.
...I suppose I shouldn't be surprised.
There was a madman here crying about some omen of misfortune. He didn't make any sense.
Where is he?
Vanished. His weiqi game remains.
Understood. I'll have someone copy it down.
One more thing. The Supervisor says Jie should be about done with the book copying, and that you probably brought the copies. Have you...?
Here.
One moment please while I count them.
Three poetry anthologies for a total of thirty-two scrolls...
Five volumes on philology for a total of sixty scrolls...
One volume on divination for a total of five scrolls...
This is... the Jijiuzhang? Looks like it got mixed in with the divination books.
Jie is such a genius. She wrote this in a single night, all one thousand and four hundred words of it, without a single character repeated.
Her calligraphy is still so beautiful. There is truly no equal.
The Candleholder heaps praise upon the scroll. Wang follows his gaze—
And sees an empty piece of paper.
I remember this... five hundred years ago, in the capital.
The first time she proposed an actionable hypothesis for destroying Sui.
She gave me the writing knife, but I don't remember what the article was about, what she wanted to tell me...
Hah. By 'repeat', did you mean making me repeat the events of the past in dream?

lines.st: Only to make you see for yourself how many fantasies have appeared in your dreams over the past thousand years, dreams about destroying me, dreams that were doomed to fail.
I should turn these memories into a cage, and drown you in the shame of countless failures.
See, now... all the memories that you have forgotten.

lines.x: You forgot?
Sorry.
It's nothing. I'm just thinking too much, if the Sui Regulator didn't say anything...
I would like to have my writing knife back.
*sigh*... I don't know why you always seem so aloof. We don't see each other very often. Are you not happy at all?
I wonder if you'd regret it, if one of us suddenly disappeared forever.
I'd rather not be called 'the Fifth Sui' my entire life. As if Its mark were still on me.
The ones who create characters helped me think of one to use as a name. Could you read this for me, brother?
I shall write it and recite it for you.
The characters '■' and '■', '■' and '■', come together to form '■'.
Mr. Wang?
Is something the matter?
I remembered something.
My sister said she did the Jijiuzhang in a hurry and asked me to check it for errors. I forgot.
Give me some time.
Not a problem.
I'll leave this one for now, then. It's getting late. You can bring it back tomorrow.
A cage of memories?
Ridiculous... You won't get your wish.
The sky has completely darkened by the time the proxy arrives at the inn.
'Ji Jiu Qi Gu Yu Zhong Yi, Luo Lie Zhu Wu Ming Xing Zi.' The Jijiuzhang is distinctive, unlike other books: it lists the names of many things and persons.
...I should have remembered.
A breeze turns the pages. Wang seems to see a piece of paper in a different color on the back cover.
She was then trying to tell me something...
Wang picks up the thin booklet from the table and opens it.

lines.st:
'Ji Jiu Qi Gu Yu Zhong Yi, Luo Lie Zhu Wu Ming Xing Zi.' The Jijiuzhang is distinctive, unlike other books: it lists the names of many things and persons.

'Fen Bie Bu Ju Bu Za Ce, Yong Ri Yue Shao Cheng Kuai Yi, Mian Li Wu Zhi Bi You Xi.' They are classified into different sections by kind, so that nothing is confused or mixed together. With but little time expended, it suffices for daily use—truly to one's satisfaction. If one studies it with diligence, one is sure to taste the delight of success.

lines.x: These should have been the words written on the first page. These were the words, when Wang scanned the page for the first time.
But now, the paper is covered in strange ink markings.

lines.st:
Sui Sui Sui Sui Sui Sui Sui, Sui Sui Sui Sui Sui Sui Sui

Sui Sui Sui Sui Sui Sui Sui, Sui Sui Sui Sui Sui Sui Sui, Sui Sui Sui Sui Sui Sui Sui

lines.x: Wang instinctively raises the writing knife and aims it at the first fierce-looking 'Sui' on the page.
One wrong move...
Who's there?
And all is lost...
The wind howls outside the window, but there is no one there.
Still trying to be our nightmare?
The same old tricks from centuries ago. Have you not grown tired of it yet?
When at a disadvantage, seek a draw...
There is no such thing as a draw between us.
When in danger, make sacrifices...
In your dreams.

lines.st: Sui

lines.x: The writing knife strikes the first 'Sui'.
Wang has used his sister's writing knife before. A slight cut, and the incorrect word is completely erased, leaving the page intact.
But he feels a great resistance, as though he is trying to scrape a boulder, a mountain even.
He tightens his not-too-strong arms and pushes down—

lines.st: Ji

lines.x: A crack of thunder outside the window.
The first 'Sui' finally disappears. The paper is blank for a moment, then the word that Jie wrote is revealed, the lines intentionally plain yet vigorous to make for easier recognition by learners.
The lone 'Ji' stands eerie amongst a page full of 'Sui'.
Is that it?
Is smearing and dabbing the worst you can do?
Do not give in to haste...
Hmph.

lines.st: Sui

lines.x: Wang takes aim at the second 'Sui' and scrapes it. The resistance seems even stronger than the first time.

lines.st: Jiu

lines.x: 'Jiu.'
The night wind invades the room, trying to turn the pages, but Wang keeps the paper firmly under his palm.

lines.st: Sui
Qi

lines.x: 'Qi.'

lines.st: Sui
Gu

lines.x: 'Gu'—
The room starts to shake violently as the fourth word reveals itself.

lines.st: Sui
Yu

lines.x: The thunder outside rumbles endlessly, accompanied by constant lightning.

lines.st: Sui
Zhong

lines.x: 'Zhong'...
A bolt of lightning strikes the inn, setting it ablaze.

lines.st: Sui
Yi

lines.x: The room burns fiercely, but Wang remains at the desk, utterly focused on cutting the page.
Each 'Sui' requires greater exertion than the last. Each time a 'Sui' is erased, a different hellish vision appears around him.
The sky sunders, the earth trembles, winds shriek and thunder roars, and fires burn red and bright.
The beams, the bed, the desk, the shelves... until there is nothing in the room left to burn, and even the ground has been burned through.
Wang can feel himself drop straight down like a stone, but his hands do not stop.
Scorching heat.
Freezing cold.
Suffocation.
Isolation...
One hundred, three hundred, five hundred, one thousand...
One thousand three hundred and ninety-three...
One thousand three hundred and ninety-four...
The last 'Sui' is erased, and he finds himself in the dark room again.

Nothing in the room has changed, except for Wang sitting in the middle of the room, his face gaunt and yellow, his clothes tattered, as though he physically journeyed through all these hellish visions.
(Heavy breathing)
What other weiqi wisdom do you have...? Spit it out, won't you?
Silence.
Wang turns the page. There is indeed a paper between the last page and the back cover. It is not words that are written on it, but a nineteen-by-nineteen weiqi board drawn with writing ink.
Not a classic match, but a handicap game of clearly mismatched sides.
Wait, this is...

lines.st: It's one thing to make me play weiqi with you because you're bored, dear brother, it's another to use the stones to form the character for 'bored.'
As if that would help me against you.
Why don't you find something else to pass the time, if it's not really a weiqi game that you're after? Go hiking, learn an instrument... I could teach you calligraphy, if it comes down to that.
Oh? What thoughts did you read this time?

lines.x: There are words on the other side...

Through the paper, Wang can see that the writing is different from the simple, straightforward lines that she used when copying for learners; it is the unique style of calligraphy that she uses when writing to her siblings.
He flips the paper over, trying to see what the message he never managed to see was—
But every word is covered by the 'Sui' character, even thicker and fiercer than the ones that he has just erased.
Same old tricks? Fine. Let's see what tricks you've got, other than illusions and parlor tricks—
Once again, Wang stabs the 'Sui' covering the page with the writing knife, wiping away a little smear on the top-right of the word...
No unusual phenomenon occurs, except the red blood that begins to flow from his right shoulder.
Urgh?!
To kill you is to kill me... is that so?
Suits me just fine...!
One stab, and the wound on his shoulder opens up.
Another stab, and the wound deepens to reach the bone.
Another stab.
And another...
Missing words? Let's see... what's the word... I can't remember...
In October, I'll be nine... nine...
Supervisor! Urgent report from the Sky Mirror Pavilion. All records pertaining to the Feranmut are—
Ugh... argh!
Perhaps the blood has gotten into his eyes, for it seems like the 'Sui' character covering the page has begun to move, as though it is alive.
The ink seems to fear the writing knife, shifting back and forth among the names of the twelve proxies...

lines.st: Wang

lines.x: It stops on the 'Wang' character.
Wang pauses and presses his bloody right side with his left hand, then resolutely points the writing knife back at the word that covers everything.
The ink is so black it seems to shine, reflecting half of Wang's silhouette.
...The last cut.
The writing knife glows bright. The proxy pours his entire spirit into it, waiting for it to strike his own name...
Haah—!
The ink on the page dissipates completely, revealing the beautiful calligraphy beneath.
But the proxy can see it no more.
He falls to the ground, covered in blood, so thin and gaunt he hardly seems human.
He has stopped breathing.

lines.st: I wanted to kill myself. I have gotten my wish.
After more than a century of plotting since your last failed attempt, this is how it ends, I the strategist, I the schemer?

lines.x: You're delusional.
The proxy stands beside his bloody corpse on the floor, perfectly unharmed as he was when he entered the Tomb of Sui.
The body on the ground dissipates like the morning dew, until only three shining black stones are left—
Until they, too, disintegrate.

lines.st: Turning yourself into weiqi stones. This was your idea.

lines.x: Three black stones in exchange for killing you once. A fair trade.

lines.st: Pathetic. You are fooling yourself.

lines.x: Fooling myself?
I've visited many of your kind. I know how incomprehensible you are... how incomprehensible we are.
I know about the faint consciousnesses that were born from the primordial void, and I know how they have transformed themselves into colossal entities in the course of an infinity.
Time, space, quantity, structure... the things that mortals take for granted, the things that seem to be everlasting, unchangeable, are merely a temporary roost for you.
I have seen one of your kind continue to disrupt what should have been the calm flow of time, even though it had died long ago and been turned into a machine at the hands of others.

lines.st: And you still dream of killing me?

lines.x: Perhaps you have no need to die. But you will fall into eternal slumber, Sui, the dreamer of evolution.
Mankind has no use for your arrogant design, and I have no use for your endless dream.
Every death in your memory is real, a genuine death of a small part of your enormous consciousness.
Don't believe me? Think back to the day I encountered that 'madman', back when your methods were so primitive they were downright laughable. What did I do?
Did I give the book back to the Sui Regulator? Did I destroy it? What was written in it? Was it really a book?
See? You've forgotten.

lines.st: You dare...!

lines.x: Your memory has died, the price you paid in exchange for three black stones.
To be precise, you died by the writing knife imbued with a proxy's entire life force.
He scraped clean every instance of the 'Sui' character across all Great Yan, and with it any trace that a creature called 'Sui' ever existed.

lines.st: The recklessness... the blasphemy!

lines.x: I would do it again, had I the writing knife in my hand right now.
I would be happy to create for you a nightmare, from which you would never wake. Even at the cost of my own life.

lines.st: ...You cannot.
The more parts of me you kill, the more you think you have weakened me, and the sooner I will wake.

lines.x: I know.
Death hurts. Each and every one of your memories is real. They are happening somewhere. The pain of your death cuts straight to the heart.

lines.st: What about you? What can you do?

lines.x: I will continue the game.
Until neither of us can ever rise again.

lines.loc: Two days before the day of the full moon, Hour of Ch'en

lines.x: *sigh*... *siiiigh*...
What are you sighing for? Does your tooth hurt?
It's my HEAD that hurts!
If you have too much time on your hands, go count the cabbage stock for winter.
Hey—
Are you going or not?
Going where, when the sun hasn't even climbed above the rooftops?
Don't play the fool with me! The city is almost empty. Why do you stay?
Almost means it's not totally empty. Travelers, soldiers on duty, they all need to eat.
Besides, we have someone right here in the restaurant.
She doesn't count!
If you don't go now, there's nothing I can do when the Sui Regulator hears of her visit to this place.
I just put the fowlbeast on the grill, and there are another four or five dishes still to be served. There's no rush.
Oh, you're just—!
Stop worrying so much, first thing in the morning. Go open the door, if you really have too much time on your hands. It's business as usual for breakfast hours!
You're bold as a boulder, you know that?
What else can I do?
If you're sleepy, go to bed. If you're hungry, eat something. Who knows when someone might suddenly miss a certain taste? A customer could show up at any moment—
See? A customer just showed up.
Take a seat you like! Only the breakfast menu is available right now—
Oh, it's you.
What are you doing here?
I'm not sure what you're asking... I run this place. What else would I be doing?
You 'people' just keep getting more and more ridiculous.
Why the rudeness? Insulting the chef before you've taken a single bite?
Keep this up, and I'll kick you out. Even my patience has its limits.
The frosty individual walks into the restaurant and sits down at the table beside Yu.
What flavors does Savors at Yu's savor?
All of them, of course, from sweet to sour to salty to spicy.
And to get the smell of fire and smoke all over yourself... do you like this?
Of course. Can't be a good cook, if you don't like to cook.
I can't speak to anything else, but I'm pretty confident in my cooking.
I could make a couple of dishes for you. On the house.
One has to taste different flavors in life. Isn't it a waste to be obsessed with a single flavor?
Ah... but I guess I can't really use these words to persuade you.
They say, 'he who tells others to forgive and forget will be struck by lightning.' Besides, I guess the bad blood between you is pretty thick.
I understand how you feel. Really!
You have every reason to hate Yan, hate humanity, hate It... and I suppose it's not out of the question for the hatred to last a thousand years.
But if you keep hating and hating, with nothing to do but let the hate destroy you... is it really worth it?
You remind me of my brother, come to think of it...
You're not afraid of me at all.
Why should I be?
Beggars, mandarins, even the gods in Heaven and the Feranmuts of legend, they're all customers when they walk into this restaurant.
You're different from the others...
You're unusual, even among your... kind.
You don't look concerned at all, even when your life is on the line.
*sigh*... I guess I've gotten used to living in fear. It's been this way for a century.
Your timing isn't great, I have to say. You would really have had a treat, if you had come when the ingredients were still fresh in the kitchen.
Sometimes, all it takes to change your mood is a nice, hot meal. Maybe the things that seemed impossible will solve themselves.
The buns are ready, chef, but our breakfast sign is gone! Did Blaze—
There's actually a customer? At this hour?
A Candleholder?
All alone... far too arrogant, you are.
Feeble, ignorant, juvenile... you and these ants.
Her sword is already raised before She has finished Her sentence.
Stop!
Chef—
Like the falling of the judge's gavel, or the crack of thunder in winter.
It is supposed to be a dining table, but it feels like a court of law.
All is silent.
The dining table is no place for swords and bloodshed.
Pick up your chopsticks, if you're here to eat. Don't let good food go to waste.
And if you're not here to eat, you ought to get out.
Its parts... protect the humans of the Sui Regulator...
How ironic.
We don't need you to tell us how to live our lives.
On the other hand, I would suggest that you eat something. Hunger tends to make one grumpy.
The woman quietly pushes a steaming plate of buns in front of Ya.
Enjoy, while it's hot.
...Hmph.
Ya looks coldly at the buns in front of Her, before turning to leave.
You're going?
The Shanhaizhong's words were truth mixed with falsehood, but I will get my hands on what she promised to deliver to me.
As for you... I've met many of Its incarnations, but none who answer my questions straightforwardly. What use is there for you people?
I will not say that an eye for an eye is unreasonable, but you should watch your step, in case someone has dug a pit for you.
Ridiculous.
Does She speak of the proxies, the Shanhaizhong, Herself, or the steaming plate of buns in front of Her?
Phew... good thing you showed up, Jun.
It doesn't seem like She's here for any particular reason. Looks like She's been tricked by someone. Whether I'm here or not doesn't make much of a difference.
It's not 'us' that She's after.
...*sigh*.
What is it?
I just feel like She must have led a hard life.
A road of hardship that only leads to even greater hardship.
Never mind that. Does my second oldest sister have business here in Baizao?
I have an appointment to keep.
An appointment? How about you sit down and enjoy a meal, if you're not in a hurry—
I'm afraid it might be a little hard to sit down and enjoy anything...
Considering the burning smell coming from the kitchen.
Oh no... my roast fowlbeast!
Stop.
The sound of paulownia seeds striking the ground rings clear through the faint morning autumn wind.
The slender woman in front of her has a nervous expression on her face, seemingly trying to hide her timidity at all costs.
A visitor? When there is a general evacuation order throughout Baizao?
Who are you? What business do you have in the Sky Mirror Pavilion?
I didn't mean to disturb—do you work here?
You must have seen the strange soldiers all over the city. They seem to have originated here.
Could you please tell me exactly what happened?
The Pavilion does not need an outsider meddling in its affairs. Besides, your identity is unknown, and you have trespassed into this place, armed.
As Magistrate of Water for the Sky Mirror Pavilion, I cannot allow you to enter, whether it is for the safety of the Pavilion's archives, or for your own safety.
Turn back.
Magistrate of Water... what kind of position is that?
The Sky Mirror Pavilion is the first library of Yan. The Magistrate of Water is responsible for preventing fires.
What are you doing in the Sky Mirror Pavilion, if you don't even know this?
I thought... a modern library wouldn't need a specific person to prevent fires anymore.
Indeed, you're right. Smart girl. Now, get out.
I'm sorry... I mean no offense.
But I happen to know someone who used to work in Baizao's library...
Who? What's your relationship?
That's not important. It was many years ago.
Hm...
The aloof woman takes a few steps forward, lifting her lantern to look closely at Ch'en's face.
Ch'en instinctively takes a step back.
Enough. Nothing you say will matter.
Leave, if you have no business here. You'll regret it when the gates close.
But you're the only one here. Are you sure you'll be fine?
That's none of your business—why do you poke your nose into it?
Honestly... I came to clear out these monsters.
But when I saw this library, I felt like... like I've seen it in a dream. Like someone intentionally guided me here.
A dream? Is that the best excuse you can come up with?
It's not an excuse. Besides, I used to be a cop. I'm familiar with security work.
Please, trust me. Let me participate in the investigation.
You... you're really a police?
Ch'en shows her ID.
You're from Lungmen...
Ch'en... Hui-chieh.
Ch'en Hui-chieh? What are you doing here?
Huh...?
Don't think a piece of paper grants you free passage.
I, Ch'en Chao-ch'ien, am the last of the Ch'ens. Have you come to mock the House of Ch'en? I'll never understand the imperial family's hobbies.
That means, you're—
Still playing dumb? Don't you even know who your parents are?
Your family never cared about the husband's side of the family when Princess Yanjing remarried, only to ruin the careers of the entire Ch'en family when she died...
What did my cousin do wrong? How could he possibly refuse?
Maybe you don't believe me... I can hardly believe it myself, but no one's ever told me so much about my family like that.
I thought... I was the only one.
You didn't know? Then why have you come to the Sky Mirror Pavilion?
They stare at each other wordlessly, as though looking into blurry mirrors.
I'm sorry...
Enough... leave. A few nice words, that's all. You were willing to say them, and I appreciate it. Let's leave it at that.
I gave up asking more questions a long time ago. Thunder and rain are both gifts from Heaven. I should be grateful I still have a job—
...Again.
Wh-What? Didn't they disappear...?
Oh no! The books!
I'll take you inside. It's faster that way. Excuse me.
Stop! Such gall—w-wait!
Insolent child—
Beneath the tall dome, densely packed bookshelves extend as far as the eye can see, reflecting in the floor like a massive, constantly expanding weiqi board.
Ink-colored scriptgeists wander amidst them, scattered like weiqi stones.
This is not good. The monsters here are much stronger than the ones I encountered at the gate.
It's like they came flowing out of the shelves... what are they?
Not shelves. Pages. Can't you tell?
Pages?
Get back!
Hey, don't grab me—
Fine, I suppose your intentions are righteous.
What do you think, Candleholder Zuo? Is the news timely enough for you?
Going straight for the source of the geist trouble—even you Sui Regulator people haven't been able to react yet.
Do you believe me now?
Shut up and give it back!
The sound of metal clanging against metal.
