Sparks fly as blades clash.
Hmph.
Mo Yi clenches her teeth as the soreness spreads from her hand to the rest of her arm.
You blockhead... gotten stronger in the span of just a few days, haven't you?
What do you want, Shanhaizhong bandit?
Don't tell me you haven't noticed something's not right with the Sui Regulator?
What about this doll? Why did you steal it?
It belongs to a proxy, just like the Book of Kung Fu. I believe... it has something to do with the vanished proxy.
As for taking it...
Would you have cooperated and come with me to the Sky Mirror Pavilion, hard-headed as you are, if I didn't?
What?!
Out of the way!
He pushes so hard that it sends the doll in Mo Yi's arms flying.
(Oh no!)
You won't—
Blades cross again in the blink of an eye.
The force sends both of them recoiling.
The blades vibrate and hum.
—Look out!
The bookshelf collapses towards Mo Yi, the shockwave forcing her back.
Stop! STOP!
(Who are they...?)
You're swinging swords at each other when there are still monsters around?! And you toppled an entire shelf of books?!
Out! GET OUT!
Wait, be careful—
Hmph...
It is unclear who moved first.
The balance is broken, and chaos breaks out.
Mo Yi turns towards the charging Magistrate of Water, angling her wrist to point her sword at her foe's shoulder.
Zuo Le's swing misses Mo Yi by inches, cutting the red rope tied to the wooden lock.
Stand back!
Ch'en has no time to pull Ch'en Chao-ch'ien back. Almost by instinct, she draws Chi Xiao, and barely parries an attack, without even seeing who made it.
Sparks fly amidst the poorly lit bookshelves, accompanied by the piercing sound of clashing metal.
Huh?
Mo Yi grabs Ch'en Chao-ch'ien by the collar and pulls her away from the scuffle.
Ch'en steps forward, her blade leading the way.
Zuo Le stands his ground—
Stop!
The girl holds up the petite form of the Magistrate of Water, and turns her blade around.
(No! The token!)
You! Give the wooden lock back!
Stop, all of you! Don't hurt the innocent!
You're... Lord Wei's niece?
Why is the token in your hands, Miss Ch'en? This is important to the Sui Regulator. You must return it at once!
Lower your weapon!
What a mess...
Miss Ch'en, return the item—
Let her go!
Return the lock!
I-I'm sure we can talk about this, calmly, like reasonable people...
I could hear you all the way from upstairs. I thought it was geists at work again.
I did not expect to see a three-person sparring session.
Mr. Liang?
I hope you're faring well, Lord Zuo.
(Lord Zuo... I've heard the name on Rhodes Island.)
I must admit, I was at my wits' end about how to resolve the trouble here...
My mind can be at ease now that the Sui Regulator has sent you to deal with it.
Well...
Do I misunderstand?
The Supervisor has her plans. There is no need to worry. I will offer what assistance I can.
Is it under the Supervisor's instructions that you are arresting this young lady?
She is Mo Yi... a member of the Shanhaizhong. She had been causing trouble at Mangshan Town a few days ago, and now she has appeared in Baizao, coinciding with the geist troubles.
This is highly suspicious and calls for a thorough investigation.
How ungrateful of you, Candleholder Zuo...
The Shanhaizhong...
(Cautiously backs off while still holding Ch'en Chao-ch'ien hostage)
The city is under martial law. I should commend Miss Mo for her boldness, if you are only here to cause trouble.
You seem duller than this Candleholder is, but I didn't expect you to be a reasonable one.
Care to persuade our Lord Zuo to listen to me before drawing his sword, for once?
My station is insignificant, and my words carry little weight. I can only ask you to release my... colleague.
'To travel across the land, and delve deeply into the laws of nature.' The original creed of the Shanhaizhong a thousand years ago. I doubt it is your intention to harm an innocent.
Mr. Liang?
How do you know that...?
...Hmph.
Mo Yi falls silent for a moment, then sheathes her sword, and gives the Magistrate of Water a gentle push.
Are you alright?
I'm fine...
Duoxie, new transcriber.
Hm...
If I recognize that sword...
You must be Miss Ch'en. It is surprising that you would return to Baizao, at a time like this.
You're Liang Xun, advisor of Yumen. I've seen you with Wei Yenwu.
You are too humble, to say that your station is insignificant.
I've left the position and am merely a humble transcriber now. Perhaps you have not yet heard the news, since you have been traveling.
(Eyes locked upon the wooden lock in Zuo Le's hand)
No sudden moves!
The token's one thing, but that lock was mine to begin with!
And what of it?
You dare?!
Speak your intentions, and I'll decide what to do with this lock.
You wouldn't believe me if I told you.
Why did the Old Tianshi allow you to come to Baizao?
Hmph...
She said that war is coming to the city, and some people will inevitably try to take advantage of the chaos... 'that young Candleholder will likely run into trouble.'
She took Deputy Minister Lin and Mr. Yun, so she wanted you to have help—
'Deal with anyone who tries to obstruct the boy, as best as you can.'
Did you think I wanted to help you, blockhead?
And what of the lock?
Still keeping secrets?
...It belonged to my late mother.
(Freezes)
I've let the hostage go, and you have my mother's heirloom. What more do you want?
...You'll still have to come with me to the Sui Regulator, to explain the previous incidents.
Cause no more trouble, and I assure you the lock will be returned to you without a scratch once all is settled.
You just want to hold me hostage with it, don't you?
Like I said, the Sui Regulator—
Good youths, now that we've resolved our misunderstandings, why don't we call a truce and clear up what's going on here?

lines.loc: Two days before the day of the full moon, Hour of Wu

lines.x: You have been in the Sky Mirror Pavilion for some time, Mr. Liang. Do you know what these soldier apparitions are?
I believe Lord Zuo is in a better position to explain it.
They are geists.
The Sui Regulator has discovered that some Feranmuts are able to animate the inanimate things, be it drawings, objects, or text.
The geists that have appeared in Baizao may be based on the Yan armies during the Great Hunt depicted in the history books in the Sky Mirror Pavilion's collection.
Their appearance likely has something to do with events at the Tomb of Sui, but it's unclear why they seem to flicker in and out of existence—
It's the breathing of the Feranmut.
'Breathing is what gives life.' The increasing frequency of geist appearances is a sign that It is waking up from Its dream.
How do you know?
I told you, you people are way behind on Feranmut knowledge.
Then why did the scriptgeists originate from the Sky Mirror Pavilion?
I'm not well-versed in Feranmutology, but I have a hypothesis concerning the Sky Mirror Pavilion...
Mr. Liang! Watch your words!
Still clinging to your stupid rules? Do you have any idea what the situation is?
It's not my intention to poke my nose into the Sui Regulator's secrets, but this is an emergency, and we need to share information.
...This place holds the only thing the vanished proxy left behind.
A writing knife.
Writing knife?
Could it be... the top floor of the Sky Mirror Pavilion, which has always been sealed shut?
It is very special. After the death of the proxy, everything that she owned, everything that she had poured her heart and soul into, disappeared with her.
But this writing knife remains.
If the geists in the city come from words, it is very likely that they have something to do with the knife.
...It all makes sense now.
What do you mean?
A little while ago, the Ministry of War sent people to search the Sky Mirror Pavilion every few days.
But the Sky Mirror Pavilion is under the jurisdiction of the Ministry of Rites. Mr. Liang has submitted several complaints to the Department of Discipline and Supervision.
They finally laid off the search these couple of days.
The Ministry of War?
Patrol Battalion soldiers tried to stop me on the main street just now...
Could this be... the Grand Commandant's doing?
Why didn't you say anything about the Patrol Battalion soldiers following closely behind that Candleholder from the Sui Regulator?
Hey...!
Come on, it's just a guess.
I never said anything about the Sui Regulator being in league with the Grand Commandant in some kind of underhanded deal.
The Sui Regulator does not indulge in faction politics.
Bureaucrats who don't engage in faction politics? That would be a first for the Baizao court.
I'll clear it up when I report back to the Supervisor!
Don't get so worked up over a mere guess.
What does the Ministry of War want with the writing knife?
What use does it have, other than its association with the scriptgeists?
A writing knife is a tool used to erase characters scribed on bamboo slips. But I know nothing about what powers the proxy's knife possesses.
You do, don't you, Lord Zuo? Go on.
The proxy assisted Yan with compiling the nation's history. Her powers were unusual.
The scrolls of the Sui Regulator record, 'With her pen, she fathoms the laws of Heaven and human affairs; with her knife, she has the power to erase and rewrite history.'
Apparently, the writing knife can erase things that... cannot and must not be hastily touched.
The name of a person, or their very existence... even the existence of an entire segment of history.
You have got to be kidding me... how can history be altered so easily?
What does altering history involve? Does it change people's memories? Or does a single word erase someone's existence?
The powers of the Feranmuts are far beyond our comprehension... we cannot interpret this through the lens of normal logic.
According to Sui Regulator research, however, the writing knife has been used for such purposes in the past.
So it seems you don't have full knowledge either.
However, the one who seeks the writing knife, the one with ulterior motives, must know how to use it.
What...?
Drat... what do we do, then?
Do we stop people with ulterior motives from getting their hands on the knife? Or do we use it to stop the geists in the city?
As far as I know, the writing knife was sealed away in the top of the Sky Mirror Pavilion many years ago, and the Sui Regulator placed strong wards on it.
Two keys are needed to unseal it. The Supervisor and the fourth proxy each hold one. The seal can only be broken if both are present.
Or maybe we can use the thing you took from the Garden of Grotesqueries?
Give it a try?
Absolutely not!
We know little of the true powers of the writing knife, and even less of this object from the Garden of Grotesqueries. We can't act rashly—
Not this, not that. Guess we'll wait for Sui to wake up and go down with the rest of Baizao.
Miss Ch'en has been quiet. What is on your mind?
What IS on your mind, Ch'en Hui-chieh?
Mr. Liang. What did you mean when you said, 'the one with ulterior motives'?
Forgive me if I'm wrong, but you are here for private matters at Lord Wei's behest, are you not, Miss Ch'en?
Where is Lord Wei?
He's missing.
Missing?!
But there are few who could possibly overcome Lord Wei in combat...
In that case... have you entered the city because you suspect that Lord Wei is already here?
Impossible! He would be accused of attempting a coup!
Wonderful. MORE sordid affairs of the big shots.
These are extraordinary circumstances, Mr. Liang. There is no need to mince words.
The writing knife, the Patrol Battalion, the Sui Regulator... do you think the unusual activities of the Ministry of War have something to do with Wei Yenwu's disappearance?
I dare not make a hasty judgment, but it's true that they were not so brazen before Lord Wei's disappearance.
As far as I know, Lord Wei and His Imperial Majesty are polar opposites. Lord Wei is decisive but sometimes rash, while His Majesty's cautiousness might be seen as indecision by some.
Perhaps the Grand Commandant's faction found Wei Yenwu's weakness and coerced him to enter the city, planning to use the writing knife to rewrite history to...
No, anyone who tried to blackmail Wei Yenwu would only suffer a hundredfold payback...
Could he willingly...?!
But it was the True Lung who summoned him back to Baizao. Unless he changed his mind at the last minute... but why hide, if he's answering an imperial summons?

Human minds are complicated things, and Lord Wei's is more so than most. If you're truly worried for him, you could start by investigating the writing knife, right here at the Sky Mirror Pavilion. Responding to change by staying still, so to speak.
Responding to change by staying still. I like that.
Where do we go then, to find out what this writing knife can do, and to protect it, Mr. Liang?
We should—
(Whispers) Mr. Liang, she—
Up.
The higher we climb in the Sky Mirror Pavilion, the more precious the tomes. They will be of great benefit.

lines.st: Pointless obsession, pointless struggle, pointless sacrifice... you have been fearful for centuries.
You divided my awakening into hundreds of cycles, each of which is subdivided by twelve, then subdivided by thirty, and then...
You live in constant fear every minute, every second, fearing the time limit, though you do not know when it will come...
Have 'I' become so foolish and shortsighted in the span of just a few dreams?

lines.x: Without a will, without a self, without someone to measure and define it, time is no more than a cycle without meaning...
As the time you spent in the tomb was.
How do you define this time, if you neither think nor feel?

lines.st: How does a dream then?
A dream hysterical for its inevitable end?

lines.loc: Year 759

lines.x: The mounts, seas, and all in between shall be our master's!
Heresy!
*pant*... *pant*...
Drop your weapon and live!
Drop my weapon?
We will teach the House of Yan that Ju has awakened, and their reign is no more than a fleeting dream compared to the infinity of heaven and earth!
The mounts, seas, and all in between shall be—
Come, drink!
Sir, I'm just a humble soldier—
Don't sell yourself short!
After centuries, we've finally eliminated the last remnant from that hunt! The lands of Yan are safe now!
A toast to all of you!
Thank you, sir!
And to the two of you, of course.
Mr. Shuo inspired the troops by always being the first to charge into the fray, while Mr. Wang's tactics were utterly flawless.
The bulk of the credit for taking down Ju must go to the two of you.
A toast to you.
...Duoxie.
This is a rare opportunity, Mr. Wang, and there is something I must say to you.
I know of the slanders against your name in the imperial court, casting suspicions upon you because you are not one of our kind.
But you have endured such slanders and made a historic contribution to Yan. Your noble heart is admirable.
To interfere even in family reunions... the Sui Regulator's restrictions on you are unreasonable.
I will petition to His Majesty to permit your family gathering...
It's a joyful day. I consider myself a strong drinker, but it appears wine has gotten the best of me...
To tell the truth, my orders were to... keep an eye on you and make sure nothing goes wrong.
But the two of you have proven yourself... utterly loyal... most respectable!
This is as far as I go... take care!
The drunken official manages a farewell and departs, helped by his bodyguards.
A good man, but a poor spy.
Did you think about what will happen to all of us if he finds out what you did?
You are drunk.
I wish I were drunk, and all this was naught but a dream.
Why did you let the beastial Ju go?
Best to have a card up one's sleeve.
Who is your opponent this time?
You must feel it. We all do... It is awakening, much sooner than the one thousand years that we expected.
We need help.
Did Ju promise to help you defeat Sui?
It is too weak to even protect the Shanhaizhong, and we have not gotten to that point. It is not time yet.
I told you. There are other ways to escape Its influence—
I know what you're going to say. You finally cleaned up the ties between you and It. You wanted 'Me' to no longer be 'Us', and 'We' no longer be 'It'...
And you, you sealed 'Yourself' away in the sword...
What then? Did the Sui Regulator return your sword?
We still live in shackles...
True shackles do not come from outside—
Do you really believe that everyone can be like you?
You remember everything and care about everything. People, events, and things from centuries past have all flowed away like the great river, but Shuo remains Shuo, always.
Not everyone can be like you...
How long would it take for Dusk to become like you? What about Yu? Do you really want Yu to be like you?
A thousand years is not enough, and we don't even have a thousand years.
We don't have time.
I'm hardly your equal in weiqi, but I know that when there is no clear path to victory in sight, it is better to play in a straightforward manner, than to attempt radical tricks without a plan.
Do you think there is no victory in this game? That you would be content with doing all the right things in an honorable defeat?
I never said that...
Your plan will have no other outcome.
What do you think we should do, then?
If you truly found a path, tell me, and I will come with you—our chances would be better that way.
Just tell me what the chances are. You can use me however you want. I would be happy to give my life.
But do you really know what you're doing?
Or has your obsession with victory blinded you to risks and consequences?
Tell me.
Say something.
Did you consider it?!
The proxy slams his palm on the stone table—or so he intended to.
But he has yet to adapt to his mortal vessel, and he misjudged both the strength and position.
A deep crack appears on the weiqi board that Wang always carries with him.
Wang, I—
Impressive, brother. This human body you created for yourself can still split boulders.
Listen to me!
No, thank you.
None of us could reach your heights, brother. You need not trouble yourself with our fates.
Wang!
What are you doing here?
Had a fight with our eldest brother? Such is written all over your face.
I get drunk by the roadside for a couple of hours, and the two of you are already making trouble.
If you have anything to say to him, you can say it to him directly.
Come now, there is no need for that.
I know what you were fighting over. Sui.
This is not the first time you have fought over this. Can you not accept your differences?
This is a matter of life and death. It's not something that can be glossed over so easily.
Would you keep fighting, even when the end comes?
Run not from the words that need to be said. The longer you delay this, the greater the problem grows.
Not today.
Wang... now, now, come back—
Weiqi failure! Can you not act your age? Playing the brat in front of your younger sister?
Ling leads the way while Wang slowly follows behind.
They see the eldest proxy still sitting by the pavilion.
Dage. Our biggest brother.
Little Ling...
I have told you, calling me 'Little Ling' makes me feel like a child.
Sorry. Old habits die hard.
So it would seem.
Hmph, smashing things in your rage? Are you the child here?
I'll fetch some good wood and make a new one for you.
Or would you prefer to keep this one? I can ask Nian or Yi to fix it, when they're here...
No need.
Oh... I recall the days when I had freshly awoken, watching you two fight amongst yourselves, time after time.
I remember the Candleholder asking me anxiously...
'How bad will it get when all your younger siblings are born? Please, give me an estimation so that I can report to the court and get a disaster response plan set up!'
Ahem.
An idea, Dage: Let me speak to Wang, alone.
I will serve as your messenger this time, in order that you need not break anything else.
...Very well.
So, about the weiqi board...
Just leave it, brother. I'll repair it myself. No need to trouble yourself.
Alright.
You are still angry.
I'm not.
You only address him as 'brother' when you are angry.
What were you arguing about? Tell me.
It is just as I thought.
His way is the proper way, but it is slow. If It were to suddenly awaken one day, it would all be too late.
As for you... *sigh*
If you want to argue with him, should you not at least come up with a plan of your own?
He gave up his sword to prove our innocence, the sword that means so very much to him. Do you feel no gratitude?
Are you perhaps... seriously furious that he smashed your board?
No...
I'm just frustrated by his lack of urgency.
We all seek a way. Dusk has even fewer ideas. Does she anger you too?
Its awakening is accelerating. We are all anxious, and it is understandable that we might provoke the occasional quarrel with inadvertent words.
But it will take centuries for It to awaken. Maintaining a cordial relationship with Yan is the more pressing matter.
Or are you saying that I, too, lack urgency?
No... that's not what I mean.
You said we're all looking for a way.
What are you looking for?
Your ears have merit.
It and I, we are all nothing but a dream.
This is just the seed of an idea, and I know not how far I can go.
But I hope to leave behind a dream.
A dream that It cannot disturb, even if It has fully awakened.

lines.loc: Year 759

lines.x: The right leg... almost fully polished.
I can't believe the first thing I'm doing after reforming my vessel is to die for someone else's sake...
It's time to go—
Oh, it's you.
No, you're It, but you're not It. You're... ah...
Interesting. Very interesting indeed.
How does it feel to see your old enemy split apart like this, and your old followers turned into an unruly rabble?
What enemy?
I simply had a dream that caused some delay. I managed to evade the Great Hunt before I awoke. It's not such a bad thing to die here after awakening.
But you should not kill me here quietly. You should let me die before the Yanese army. Otherwise, maniacs will keep coming, trying to die in my name.
It would be the last thing I want.
Desperate people will resort to desperate measures. You are not necessarily the reason.
I won't kill you. Quite the opposite. I can't let you die.
You want to help me instead of killing me? Interesting.

How do you intend to deal with the self-proclaimed 'Shanhaizhong'? Though it is no more than a fragment, they have indeed inherited the old carpenter's arts. They are not entirely unassociated with me.
They'll live, if you leave.
What do you mean?
The Yanese army wants you, not them. It's trivial for you to leave.
I'll help you, if you encounter any obstacles.
As for the Shanhaizhong, they'll last two more days, give or take. Ultimately, they will all die, taking a third of the Yanese army with them.
If you die for them here, they will become even more fervent and fight to the bitter end.
If you leave, they will flee, and Yan's mission will be done, so they can recall their armies.
This is the only way for you to stop the war.
You are It... no, not quite. You are ten times It in scheming and intrigue.
I would be indebted to you. What do you wish from me in return?
A piece in reserve that could perhaps come in useful. No great loss, if it never comes into play.
Now that makes me feel a little slighted.
I'll repay this debt. I promise.
How do you intend to help me, when you don't know what I'm trying to do?
You would treat a Feranmut as a piece in reserve. What you're plotting must not involve mortal man. This is about yourself. Split apart, you said? How many pieces are there?
...Twelve.
Then I take it the only thing you care about is whether it is the twelve that is left, or the one.
One word of advice. One tree does not make a forest. You are defying the laws of nature. You need all the help you can get.
Ask your siblings if they've thought of any alternatives. Beings like you are not meant to be measured by normal logic and reason.
If you can find the solution, you will rid the world of a great threat...
We will never be truly gone, as long as my dream has not been fully worn away, as long as the twelve of us persist in my dream.
Falsehood is reality, the Great Way unfettered. Such is for all things.
It sounds to me you intend to carry the heaviest yoke of them all.
You want to drag the entire family into the instant before Sui awakes, then extend the instant into eternity in your waking dream...
How long do you think you will last?
What if I drank all the wine across all the lands?
I hope there is a less desperate way.
We were never equals to It. Desperate times call for desperate measures, do they not?
I know. But...
...I did not save Ju on a whim.
Why do you bring this up again? Of course you have your reasons for doing things, but too often you cast your eyes too far and forget the things that are near...
Wait, what do you mean?
I was asked to do so.
By whom?
Hou.
The old man's alive?
He has avoided the eye of Yan, as he does not have followers like the Shanhaizhong. He told me about Ju after I took him beyond Yanese reach.
Have you told our eldest brother?
Not yet.
You can be pretty astute, when you need to be.
I asked Hou about ideas for a way out, after I helped Him leave Yan. His words have given me thought.
'Perhaps I am not in Its dream. Perhaps It is in my dream.'
Interesting, that old man. I would love to drink with Him, if I got the chance.
The unusual activity of Sui may indicate that something is disturbing It in its dream and accelerating Its awakening.
It's merely addressing the symptoms, but if I can enter Its dream and see what the culprit is...
Risky. Are you not concerned It may awaken?
Does it really make a difference, whether It awakes in seventy years, or seven days?
What if I offer not my help?
I'll find a way.
Might this way have something to do with me? Such as, stealing something from me?
I will take that as a 'yea'.
...*sigh*.
Give me your word, you will not do anything too foolish in this dream.
If I violate my oath—
Stop.
There is no need for oaths between family.
But please, do not break my heart.
Yes.
Now then, drink up. You may take the goblet with you.
Just drink?
What more do you need to dream? No ritual necessary.
The elder proxy takes the cup and downs its contents.
As vertigo overcomes him, he attempts to support his body with his arms.
The stone table beneath his elbows had turned into a mountain boulder covered in moss.
He has never seen such scenery, be it in the chaos, or after he came into the mortal realm.
He is It, but Its dreams are too complex to be closely examined one by one.
Where am I...?
A battlefield.
A battlefield of void and emptiness.
The proxy hears Feranmut roar at Feranmut, humans swing their weapons at other humans, humans swarm Feranmut, before they are suffocated, crushed, and devoured.
He hears the pained trembling of Feranmuts, as massive human weapons are locked and loaded...
But he sees nothing.
Invisible Feranmuts walk across the ground. Vegetation disappears where they tread, revealing black and yellow soil.
Invisible entities strike the mountains, cracking them and causing avalanches.
But It remains still.
It is in fear, angry, in pain, but It cannot fight back, cannot avoid it, not even let out a whimper.
Is this Its... nightmare?
After the twelve of us left, Its dreams lost their visible image...
The proxy takes a step back, but even the mountains are trembling in the nightmare.
He can hardly keep himself upright—
Just because you're in a dream doesn't mean you shouldn't watch your feet.
Who's there?!
How did you get here?
Ju wanted to come personally to help you, but His brain has a hard enough time dreaming for Himself, much less entering another's dream. I'm more suited to these things.
Lamentable, is it not, that Sui's dream has become so torn and broken... fortune and misfortune are truly two sides of the same coin.
You are in Its nightmare, and you have drawn its lines, like it or not.
Look.
Hou points in the distance, where heaven and earth melt into one. Suddenly, a black silhouette emerges from nothingness.
Feranmut.
Humanity.
Weapon.
It all seems utterly unreal, like the picture books that some Yanese soldiers carry with them.
Flip through the pages rapidly, and the pictures begin to move, as though they have been subject to Dusk's touch.
I'll awaken It?
Quite the opposite.
A nightmare does not last long. Anyone trapped in a nightmare only seeks to wake up. This silhouette at least allows It to sleep a little longer.
Then, what do you mean by fortune and misfortune being two sides of the same coin?
Hou says nothing, but Wang sees it.
At first, the images are awkward and stiff, but soon they become smooth.
Not much has changed, except for a white shadow appearing within the black silhouette.
What is that?
All twelve of you took something from Its dream. When It let Yu go, Its old dream gradually blurred, and the new dream became empty.
But what was left behind was not a void. It was a mirror, waiting to reflect the first thing that enters this dream.
That thing... that man, is you.
What is this?!
You are seeing Its nightmare, and now a reflection of you—the you who filled the void in Its dream.
You told me about the strange things that happened to your family a century ago. Now, they have all come together... but in your image.
This is Its creation, Its puppet, and... the reflection of a reflection, which It is loath to face.
It's time to go.
Go?
What else? Do you intend to kill It?
If this is the shape of the void, then killing it might destroy whatever consciousness It still retains in the dream.
Your mind is sharp, but your words are terrifying.
You could not kill Sui outside the dream. What makes you think you can kill It in the dream?
I suppose, you could try it, if you are confident that you will be able to escape unscathed.
I'm confident... that I can take it down with me.
You don't listen to what people say, do you?
I suppose I'll have to fetch someone whom you care about.
Wang?
He wants to sacrifice his own life to kill Sui in this dream.
Have you abandoned your word so soon, my second-oldest brother?
Such opportunity...
Will not come again—
That's quite enough. It's time to bring your brother out of the dream, girl, or we'll all be in danger—
In this dream, this time.
I glimpsed the dream of your slumber, and my own reflection.

lines.st: You saw the reflection, and the price was... to create an inexplicable foe for yourself in the centuries to come.
Having to face this again, you must be filled with regret.

lines.x: What a terrible error. If I knew this reflection of yours could leave the dream and enter reality... I would not have left so easily.
Where are you hiding?
No matter... I will find you.
Yes. It's time to go.
Clearly unwilling to stay any longer, Hou waves His stick, and the three figures disappear...
But neither He nor Ling realizes that they have simply taken one of many weiqi stones.
The stone will shatter when they depart the dream, and Hou and Ling will not find the same hill when they attempt to return to the dream.
Don't try to find me again.
It's not worth it.
A black aura suddenly surrounds the proxy. The next instant, he has leapt from the cliff, into the formation of thingless things.
At the horizon, the beast-shaped silhouette seems to have been hit by something, letting out a wail that shakes the earth.
The grey-white tide of humans outlined by black lines quickly swallows the Feranmut's silhouette and rushes toward the proxy.
In the distance, it looked like a thin line; up close, the raging tide obscures the heavens.
Black armor, plain robes, iron swords, pale bows.
Yanese equipment from a thousand years ago in black and white, like an uncolored draft, surrounding the proxy.
The first soldier charges at the proxy. A spear carrying the afterimage of Arts rushes towards the proxy's throat. The proxy cleaves it in two.
Bring it on!
He shouts.
The army in Sui's dream pauses for an instant...
Then, they rush towards him, like a river that his words had frozen for an instant.
What's going on?
M-Mr. Shuo...
You're Ling's Candleholder? Where is she?
As he speaks, a claw the size of a mountain is brought down upon Shuo.
Shuo swings his fist. Heaven and earth tremble.
Its attack foiled, the Sui beast turns its attention to the other side, at the enormous weapon that has just been assembled, not even taking the time to lick its bleeding wounds.
We can't stop It...
Half the Imperial Guard have fallen, along with the Yumen troops that had been deployed to the Tomb of Sui...
We have mobilized every soldier in the four cities around Baizao. We can only hope, before It destroys the next city...
Where is Wang... where is he?!
What use is there in looking for him?
Ling took the others into a dream to protect them when Sui awoke. You led the army to fight Sui... who else could have awakened It, other than the great offender?
The... offender...
Dage!
Ling?!
You should not be here! Or has the dream already...?

Who could see... a gulper of cups...
Become now...
Ling's eyes are closed. The dream has yet to end.
Even in the dream, however, she and the other siblings in it can feel Its presence, so very near now.
It has come to this...
...A fight to the death, it is.
Shuo is awake. He draws his sword, which has not yet tasted blood.
In the dream, Ling points her lantern at the body of the bestial Sui.
The bestial Sui turns around, towards the beings born of Itself—
Yet just before everyone on the battlefield—every human and every beast—could plunge into the coming slaughter, a bloody, black figure appears behind the bestial Sui.
Caught you.
You—
Through dreams in dreams infinite, all of me and all of us are here. There is nowhere for you to go.
You wouldn't dare! Kill me, and you will be destroyed, as will all your family—
Wang does not answer. He lunges forward with his sword and impales the throat of his own reflection, which he had pursued through dreams far too numerous to count.
Strange scenes from countless dreams gush from the wound.

Mountains, Feranmuts, armies... white dreams boil and roil between heaven and earth, and a white mist, fever-hot like fresh blood, wraps around the army and proxy outside the dreams, even the Feranmut.
Only when the white figure falls to the ground do the others see that two bodies lie upon the ground, all life gone from them.
One was Wang. The other was exactly like Wang, but not even death could wipe the malice from his face—no one knew who he was.
Ling?!
As the two bodies fall, Ling and her siblings in the dream vanish into nothingness.
Ling!
The bestial Sui wails with the last proxy.
You're still trying to daunt me with illusions... it will not succeed.
'Who could see a gulper of cups, become now a sorrowful drunk?'
Ling would never say that, not even with the end upon us.

lines.st: ...How many of your stones sacrificed, this time?

lines.x: Go on, count.
You may think that I don't have many stones left, but how much is left of your memories?

lines.st: Not much.
But perhaps you have forgotten something.
Did you really think a few fragments, split from a mere one-twelfth, could possibly stand against me—the whole that was born alongside this entire land?
Remember?
Your last failure was the same as this.

lines.loc: Year 962

lines.x: This is as far as we can go, Your Imperial Majesty.
You are the only one in all of Yan who may enter the room beyond this door.
It is but a door. There is no need to be so fearful.
It conceals Yan's greatest secret, and its greatest asset, Your Imperial Majesty...
...Is that so?
The seventeen-year-old True Lung walks down the last few steps.
He looks back at the three Grand Ministers behind him, who are still bowing deeply. He frowns and pushes the door open.
He has heard about what lies behind the door. The legends and stories of this ancient nation that have been passed down through the centuries—
'The Earth's blessings have granted this stone to its people; the Heaven's blessings shall bring an end to Sui.'
Some say that the prosperity of Yan comes from it. Others say the True Lung's authority is merely borrowed from it.
But no one has seen it with their own eyes in nearly a century.
The ascending True Lung is filled with excitement, his fingertips trembling.
Everything in the land of Yan shall obey him, for the prosperity of Yan—
...So will one small crystal.
He pushes the door open.
As ambitious as he is, he could not help but stop before the massive space and blinding light.
It is not the first Originium itself, but man-made additions upon it, the limiters and guidance that generations after generations of Tianshis have placed upon it over the centuries.

lines.st: At the new capital of Baizao, a tomb was built to hold the vessel of Sui, and the consciousness of Sui was suppressed by the power of calamity. Yet the Emperor was not at ease, and so he sought counsel from the Tianshi Bureaus in hopes of keeping the peace for eternity hence.

Then the Emperor discovered the means to harness the power of calamity, but it would take the lives of a hundred Tianshis and ten thousand soldiers to form the blade and slay the beast. Even if complete success were achieved, Baizao would not be spared.
And the Emperor said, 'It is shameful to bring harm to the people for one's own purposes. I shall give up myself, and my descendants shall do the same, and not demand the sacrifice of the people.'
The True Lung passed the next year, and by it the enchantment was complete. The crown prince grieved and named it 'Bu Fan'.

lines.x: ...Bu Fan, 'No Rebellion'. Sounds like 'No Return' too.

My ancestor sacrificed himself and made the reigning True Lung the subject of future human sacrifice. Henceforth, the True Lungs that came after him gave their lives to harness the power of this Originium.
I would not have known it was so massive if I did not see it for myself...
Yet it's no more than a tiny crystal compared to all of Yan and Yan's future.
Yan could have done much more with this object.
How much energy have we spent on suppressing the sleeping beast? With such energy, how many armies could we have raised, how many machines could we have built?
How could I tolerate such... inaction?
The True Lung lingers before the facility for a while. He has gotten used to the scorching light, and even starts to feel... boring.
A smile comes to his face as he remembers the reverence on his three Grand Ministers' faces. How will he deride their timidity when he leaves the room without being rude?
He recalls the 'rule' that kept the ministers out, for him to face this structure alone.
The rule that inextricably links it to his life.
He could not help but laugh.
The True Lung's authority over all under heaven, tied to a tiny crystal like this?
I am still inexperienced. I will be relying on the three of you for many things.
Yan has been peaceful and prosperous for centuries. Why is it that there are still rebellions?
Is there still land where the blessings of Yan do not touch? Or seditious subjects who will not submit to imperial rule?
Silence.
I will decide myself, then, as you do not answer.

The blessings of Yan shall touch every corner, so that not a single person shall worry for food or shelter. The teachings for Yan shall reach every heart, so that not a single rebellious thought will enter the mind of a single man.
We shall vanquish the northern traitors and build the nomadic city of Baizao. Both are the foremost priority, and neither shall be delayed.
You shall summon the Six Ministries, discuss the specifics, and deliver a proposal by the end of the month.
Dismissed.
The trio on the other side of the curtain bow in assent and leave.
A little later...
Ah, you have arrived.
...It is an honor, Your Imperial Majesty.
I've long heard of the twelve 'extraordinary talents' of Yan, credited with no small number of achievements for the empire. I've been curious for some time.
I therefore perused the Sui Regulator's archives, and found something rather interesting.
Be it reports of triumph, or suspicious activities...
No name has been mentioned more often than yours.
But I see your visage is not as terrifying as the rumors claim.
Why has Your Imperial Majesty summoned me?
They call you the most cunning strategist in all Yan.
How do you propose to resolve the two great obstacles we face?

The northern rebellion is hardly a major threat. The rebels may be influential in their local area, but they are powerless to expand. Nevertheless, it would be inadvisable to allow them to consolidate their position.
Fifty-thousand soldiers and six months' worth of rations will be sufficient to defeat them.
Your estimate is much more optimistic than the Ministry of War... but I have more reason to trust your judgment on this.
What about the other matter?
As for the modification of Baizao into a nomadic city... I understand the Ministry of Works has validated the engineering principles. The only issues are funding, supplies, and...
...the Tomb of Sui beneath Baizao.
Yes. You understand the problem.
It is for this that I have summoned you.
How do you intend to eliminate Sui?
If you are familiar with the scrolls of the Sui Regulator... you understand that 'we' were the same and one as Sui, and our fates intermingled.
Do you mean to ask how to kill 'us'?
It is only 'Sui' that Yan is concerned about, and no other being.
You understand, of course, the price that Yan paid for the hunt that occurred centuries ago.
The price that must be paid, if Yan is to force the issue, is seven-tenths of its armies, one-tenth of its population, and fifty years of unceasing unrest.
If that is the case, Yan will be in no position to ensure the safety of the twelve of you.
The proxy looks up and meets the gaze of the man behind the curtain.
I am determined to cleanse Yan of this thousand-year bane. I am not afraid to pay the price—any price.

The Sui will be destroyed. The tremendous energy that was used to suppress that beast shall be reutilized to build a hundred cities and ten thousand battleships, and usher in another thousand years of prosperity for Yan.
I had hoped that there would be a place for the twelve of you in Yan's future prosperity, on account of your past deeds. It is regrettable.
If your decision to eliminate Sui comes from practical judgment, rather than reckless pursuit of glory...
...I may have a way.
Oh?
The twelve of us were one and same as Sui. We were destined to fade, whether It awakes or is destroyed.
But it is possible that I could... replace It.
How sure are you?
I will need time...
The young man behind the curtain points at the courtyard outside.
That ginkgo sapling was planted in the spring of this year.
The Ministry of Works swore an oath in this hall several days ago that the rebuilding of Baizao into a nomadic city will be complete before the tree bears fruit.
Twenty years. That is the time you have.
That is too little time.
I am not here to bargain.
Immortality has made you arrogant. Perhaps you have forgotten that mortals do not have many 'twenty years'.
Yan cannot wait, and neither can I.
You're the new Candleholder? So much time has passed, in the blink of an eye.
Has the previous Candleholder...
She retired last month, and asked me to tell you not to worry.
I will take over your communications with the Sui Regulator from now on.
Well, you'll have your hands full.
Most Candleholders have to worry about tracking down my free-roaming siblings. As for mine...
They have to sit in this office all day, handling books and scrolls. They are more like secretaries than Candleholders.
I enjoy reading and writing. This is right up my alley.
Perfect.
What's your name?
I'm Chun.
'Chun'?
'For the ancient tree that shares your name, eight thousand years is a spring and eight thousand years is an autumn...' Was the one who named you remarkably ambitious, or remarkably cautious?
Wait, you are...?
...An Elf?
Yes.
Judging from your appearance... you're an Elf of Yaan?

The bestial Yaan and its Elves are well-versed in the ways of Yin-Yang and the Five Elements. The element associated with spring is wood, and the character for 'Chun' combines both 'wood' and 'spring', representing vitality and longevity... I see.
My mother told me about it when I was young. I didn't expect anyone else to know.
Stuff like this is what history is about.
It's your first day. I won't put you to too much hard work. Just familiarize yourself with the space; you can look at any books and documents in this study.
The Candleholder carefully scans the room. She could not conceal her eagerness as she sees scrolls and books that fill the wall shelves.
Did you write all of these?
No, I simply collect the materials. The historians of the Imperial Historical Cabinet are the ones who actually write the books.
After all...
Is writing history a difficult task?
It's a heavy responsibility.
History books form people's perception of the past, but a few words are hardly enough to give them a complete view.
Choices must be made as to which parts to record for posterity, what sorts of lessons those who come after should learn...
It's easy to say, 'write with an impartial pen,' but it is a most profound and complex discipline.
What if there is a mistake in the history books?
Then it should be corrected.
History is inevitably warped by the position of the narrator, no matter how impartial the writer attempts to be.
I see a few more problems because I've lived longer than most and remember more than most.
Most... it's true that your kind, the proxies, are different from other long-lived. Before I joined, I heard that each of you had your own 'powers'.
Do you use your powers to correct the errors in the history books?
I heard you have a magical writing knife... do you use it to correct those errors?
It can, but this is not how it should be used.
...Huh?

Mistakes take root in people's hearts as they are passed down from one generation to another. The proper way to fight them is to form a new consensus through debate and clarification, not to simply erase it.
Erasing a wrong word from one's own writing is not a significant issue, for it only concerns oneself.
To do it to a published book would be to meddle with the memories of thousands.
As for history... setting aside the question of how much I can alter, it will not be a good thing, for myself and for Yan.
Frankly, it's already a chore to ensure that history is not altered. I hardly have the energy to alter it myself.
Hm...
Seems like there's something you would like to change with the knife yourself.
Huh?! I—
Before my mother passed away, she said I may be the last of the Yaan Elves.
She... she wanted me to find a home.
I wandered for many years. I tried many ways to blend in, but I always failed. Yan does not want—
Yan does not want to accept you, because the Yaan Elves were depicted in the Great Hunt as... 'fickle and disloyal.'
But I know that's not true...
The bestial Yaan stood against Yan simply to survive. That cannot be denied.
The Elves, though, had only wanted to mediate the conflict, yet they became the enemy of both sides... ultimately, most were killed in battle, and their deeds lost to time.
So you wanted to alter this history?
I don't deny that I thought about it. But I'm your Candleholder now, and my work comes before all else.
I can't lend you the writing knife, nor can I allow you to use it to alter history.
I understand. I shouldn't have—
But if we can find proof that the Elves were in fact not disloyal, I will do all that I can to help you correct the erroneous records, in the proper manner.
Huh?
Things haven't been too busy lately. We could visit the area where you used to live. What do you think?
I... I've only just come. Besides, the things I said aren't necessarily true. I—
I can tell that your passion for words and history is genuine. Besides... who will believe you, if the historian does not?
Yes... yes!
This is the key to my office. You're my Candleholder now. Take good care of it.
How do you get in, if you give me the key?
Why should the master of the house need a key?

lines.loc: Year 965

lines.x: The court building has only just been completed! Why...?
Don't worry, it will not be torn down. Our Tumu Tianshis will transplant the Judicial Review Office to the nomadic plate, undamaged.
But wouldn't it have been so much cheaper to build it directly on the nomadic plate?
I hear the imperial court is putting everything behind this 'nomadic city'. I'm sure our cooperation will be very important.
Get to the point. What do you want?
My rosewood chair, ebony table, Kou-wu plates and Danyan lamps... none of them were inexpensive.
Here are the receipts from when I ordered my things. If there is any damage...
Fine, fine. You'll be reimbursed accordingly.
Yes, the prisoners have been transferred.
The previous instructions were from the Judicial Review Office, while the new ones are from the Ministry of Justice?
I understand, but am I supposed to conjure the prisoners out of thin air?
You don't care? The burdenbeast carriages are arriving in minutes?
Minister. Please excuse the poor hospitality. I did not know you were visiting.
I have never seen you in the Sky Mirror Pavilion. I take it you have important business?
There isn't much time, Miss Jie. I'll get straight to the point... there is an error in this year's archives that I would like to ask you to correct.
A mistake in the documents that I audited? Pray tell.
The documents submitted by the Ministry of Works state there were around a hundred deaths associated with the Chidao project at Danyan this year. Why did the number become three thousand?
The figure of three thousand deaths was not cited from a single document, but calculated based on documents from the Ministry of Works, the Ministry of Revenue, and local government departments.
The records are carefully sourced. I have kept a copy, should you wish to review them.
While it is not my place to question the work of the Ministry of Works, I have no idea why the three thousand deaths in Danyan's local records became just a hundred when it reached Baizao.
You must submit sufficient evidence, however, should you wish to question the documents audited by the Sky Mirror Pavilion.
Ahem... well, Danyan is not so well-staffed as Baizao, and there are only limited resources available for record-keeping.
Besides, the cold climate there causes disease and frostbite in winter. Perhaps not all of the three thousand died in the course of constructing the Chidao...
I see. You wish for me to alter the documents to make the Ministry of Works look better before the cabinet.
This is not about the cabinet. This is about Yan.
You know about all the projects being carried out across Yan. They push the nation's resources to the limit, but there is no doubt that these projects will benefit the nation and its people.
I'm not the only one who feels that such... depressing figures will weaken the people's morale.

I don't deny the benefits of these measures. Precisely because of that, the three thousand people who died to build the Chidao gave their lives for their country. It would be an insult to their memory to reduce it to a hundred.
Unless you can prove that there has been an error, any text that I have reviewed must not be altered. Anyone else who has a complaint can bring it to me directly.
The nation must be united in the monumental task of building the nomadic city. Everyone at the court is doing their best, and each faces their own challenges...

Yet the writings of the Imperial Historical Cabinet and the Sky Mirror Pavilion have been most... aggressive and deprecating. It is as though you wish for work on the nomadic city to stop immediately!
Is it the writer who has something to hide, or the reader?
How dare you...!
The imperial court has compensated the next of kin in full! Not a single coin has been withheld!
I have noted this in the documents that I approved. The care that the imperial court has shown towards those injured and killed, as well as their families, illustrates Yan's love towards its people.
Then why do you cling to a single number like this?!
I understand your concern for Yan, minister. But I worry that people will only see the glory of the accomplishments, and not the people whose sacrifices made it possible.
All I can do is to ensure that the history I see is recorded exactly as it is.
And you think you alone can decide the history of Yan?
History has never been decided by one person. It is the combined soundless voices of millions.
You are a learned man in a position to improve the lives of thousands upon thousands, minister. I simply leave behind some words for the people whose voices will never be heard.
Now, ask your conscience, and answer again for those people...
How many died building Danyan's Chidao this year? Was it a hundred... or three thousand?
(Mutters irritably)
The Minister of Works just stormed off in a huff... is it because of Danyan?
Is this why you have come, sister?
I can't imagine how they managed to convince you to lobby on their behalf... am I actually dreaming, while the one in the Tomb of Sui has awakened?
Are you so sarcastic to everyone these days?
I came to tell you that the Sui Regulator and the Ministry of Justice have approved your request to clear the Yaan Elves' name.
I'm sorry. I was in a bad mood.
Apologies accepted.
That's the first good news I've heard in days. It will make Chun less gloomy, I hope.
It's not good to be fighting with the Sui Regulator, but I doubt they would be happy to see you get too friendly with your Candleholder.
I understand. But she's really taken to her work, helping me compile the histories... she clearly likes it.
I have a feeling... she didn't become a Candleholder because she wanted to, but because she was alone and didn't have a choice but to take the hand that was offered.
Well, a wrong has been righted. That's always a good thing.
Of course, I think part of their intention is to get you to compromise on changing the documents.
I simply came to discuss matters, because this matter has gotten to the three Grand Ministers and the True Lung. I'm not lobbying on anybody's behalf.
Just treat it as the guy in the Tomb of Sui throwing a tantrum.
You're the only one in the family who jokes about this.
Ling has honeyed words, and you speak with heaven's authority. I'll settle for the frivolous tongue, then.
It's true that the officials of the court are facing a dilemma in this matter.
The figure of three thousand will cast a shadow upon the nomadic city project, and the implications it may have for Yan are unknown.
Did you tolerate the falsification of records while you were still administering justice?
Falsifying records is a crime, of course. However, if the perpetrator did not do it out of selfish interest, I will consider extenuating circumstances when determining the punishment.
I'm surprised to hear that.
We each have our own specializations. I don't work in history, and you don't work in law.
Perhaps that is how matters of law work... but not in history.
I wanted to study history and language because I wanted to learn what the human mind is truly like.
The answer to my questions may lie in the intention behind words, and the truth behind history...
Not for Yan, nor for the land, not even for our family... only for my own questions—
Jie! Jie!
Chun? What are you doing here? I wasn't ready to tell you—
Tell me what?
Never mind... what's the hurry?
I went to the study to get the drafts as you asked, but I discovered signs of an intrusion!
The writing knife on the desk is gone!

lines.loc: Year 965

lines.x: ...I see.
Do not tell anyone else. Be extremely careful.
I need you to do something for me.
The Judicial Review Office has begun to investigate everyone who was near the Sky Mirror Pavilion around the time that the writing knife was stolen.
Outside of our siblings, you could count the number of people who know about the existence of the knife with one hand.
Yes, it's just as I feared.
I won't stand aside and do nothing if someone tries to harm you, Jie.
I doubt someone trying to harm me would use such means.
It feels more like a warning.
His Imperial Majesty is an energetic ruler. He wanted every 'person' in the land to bow to him. He would recognize our family as not being of his kind.
But I do not believe he would stoop so low as to use the knife in underhanded ways.
It's not fair. If there's anyone who should be above suspicion, it would be you.
You know how hard the words 'fair and just' are to write... it's as our oldest brother says. There is little point in arguing when it's a thousand mouths against one.
I doubt he would just let this slide, if he was here.
Hehe...
I wouldn't like to see him when he's angry.
What is your plan? You won't tell Wang about it?
It's times like these that he is most useful.
Jie shakes her head.
That was what I was about to ask you... please, keep this a secret from Wang.
People have feared and avoided him for many years. His position has grown even more precarious under the new emperor. This is not the time to drag him into this.
Besides, I fear he will do something irrational if he finds out.
Alright, I'll listen to you.
But I hope you can be honest with me, Jie.
Are you absolutely sure that you and Wang are not plotting something?
Come on, don't interrogate me like this, sister.
I guess no one can lie to you successfully.
I knew it.
You understand that it's a dangerous power you wield.
I know the risks. But I'm not the only one who has to shoulder them.
Shuo, Wang, Ling and you... you've done so much for us the younger ones. I have to do my part.
Besides, I don't want these names that I put so much thought into to fade away in history.
Jun continues to frown. Jie takes her sister's hands in hers. They are rough from all the years of playing instruments, but it is a comfortable feeling.
I'm here. I'm alright.
Don't worry...
You know, I had a dream, a dream about our future.
It's a different future from the fields of white that Shu spoke of.
I saw all of you truly live as yourselves.
The vision was so real, it didn't seem like a dream. I even have a feeling...
A feeling that it's more than just a dream...
It's good to see you, Miss Nian.
Hey, Round-head. You're the one supervising the work?
What about the 'foreman'? Where's he slacking off?
You misunderstand. My master has been very busy with city design ever since he received news of Baizao's modification. He has gone an entire month without sleep at his busiest.
That slowpoke picks himself up once in a while, eh?
He calls Yan's first-ever nomadic city a project of monumental significance. Every detail of the city must be carefully considered.
Therefore, he must complete it before you have a chance to do something to his design.
What the—!
How dare he talk behind my back like this, when I always share the good stuff with him?!
Where is he? I'll bite his head off for this—
Sleeping.
He has slept for ten days now, but he is supposed to deliver the draft of the gates to the Ministry of Works today.
He told me that I am to stop the ministry's people from getting in, no matter what.
Ha! Now that's the Yi I know.
Is there something else?
You said Yi's sleeping...
I remember that he picked up a meteorite a few decades ago... it's still in his garden, isn't it? Where?
Miss Nian... I have my master's instructions. I am not to allow anyone into the Garden of Grotesqueries, whether it is you or anyone from the Ministry of Works.
Why are you so loyal to him? Say, wasn't it I who gave him the materials to build your body? You should obey MY orders!
Now shut up and get out of the way!
Please, Miss Nian... you make my position most difficult—
Mr. Liang, Miss Nian. The Assistant Minister requests your urgent presence at the Forbidden City.
Urgent? How much?
The Baizao project has encountered a snag...
A shipment of materials, Oriron and Originium and the like, which Baizao urgently needs, has been delayed.
It has brought construction to a grinding halt.
What the...
What are the geezers at the Ministry of Works doing?
What is going on... I demand an explanation!
The construction of Baizao is the most important thing in the land! How could this be allowed to happen?!
The Chidao around the capital is well-maintained. How could it collapse when no Catastrophe has occurred? And how could there be bandits so close to the capital?
I have to say that these incidents are highly suspicious, sir.
The sudden collapse, and the supplies that disappeared without reason, then showed up just as suddenly where they should not...
If there is indeed someone behind all this, then their objective must be to sow confusion.
Rumors have been spreading in the city. Many workers believe that Baizao picked a time of ill fortune to begin construction.
Someone is sowing confusion? Who?
Sir, the Minister of Rites requests a meeting.
What is Rites doing here at a time like this? Did they come to laugh at us?
Reporting, sir: the Minister of Rites said that although the Ministry of Works has been quite busy of late, this trouble involves more than just the Ministry of Works...
There you are, Chun.
Do you know why I have summoned you?
I suppose... it's because of the writing knife.
Yes.
How do you propose to solve this dilemma?
Perhaps, if you could... put in a few good words for her, and return it to its owner...
Ha.
Go on.
If the numbers are the problem, it's as simple a matter as going over her head to alter them... this should not be necessary.
How interesting. Her previous Candleholder retired after being accused of growing too close to her proxy... she has a knack for winning people over, doesn't she?
She... I mean, all the proxies have their own eccentricities. But they have never plotted against Yan—
Indeed, we can tolerate the eccentricities of others, as long as they do not seek to harm Yan.
But she...
You must understand what you are dealing with, and what her powers mean to Yan.
The Supervisor tosses the unsubmitted document towards Chun, and hands her a writing brush dipped in ink.
Try.
Try, if you think you can go over the head of the proxy. Change the numbers to what you want them to be.
Your Imperial Majesty... the Ministries of Rites and Works have completed the investigation into the lost supplies for the Baizao project.
The investigators unanimously agree that it was most likely an intentional act... and that there was someone behind it.
The supplies have arrived at Baizao. The Ministry of Works will do its best to make up for lost progress...
What are you hiding?
You suspect that there is someone behind it. You must have a suspect.
The nervous official dares not look straight at the figure behind the curtain. Out of the corner of the eye, he glimpses his majestic lord standing before his desk, writing something.
A well-timed declaration of war. Two, in fact.
One, telling me that she is the sole mouth of Yan, while the other demonstrating that he can easily disrupt Yan's greatest project, that the continuation of the project depends on his approval.
Will there come a day when they could make Yan's armies turn on themselves if they wanted?
This is a most serious matter, Your Majesty. If the proxies are indeed stirring trouble, the Sui Regulator should act immediately and punish them severely.
There is no need to be hasty.
The figure behind the curtain thinks for a moment, then puts down his writing brush.
I know the craft of calligraphy must be honed over a long period, but I have not seen the efforts bear fruit...
Still, I understand now why the Grand Preceptor wanted me to spend so much time on it.
One must be in control of oneself.
The young True Lung gazes upon the ink on the paper, but he seems dissatisfied and frowns.
'Jin Ye Ming Yue Ren Jin Wang'—The moon's wan gloom drew every eye.
He picks up the writing knife and uses it to scrape away the conspicuous character of 'Wang'.
In an instant, that patch of paper is white as new again.
He is clever, unlike his pedantic sister. He knew to let the supplies arrive at Baizao eventually, make a show of good faith.
He wished to protest on behalf of his sister. I'll humor his brotherly love. They can record the figures as they like.
As for this writing knife...
I had considered doing something with it, but...
Never mind. Return it.
Jie...
The Sui Regulator has recovered your item.
Thank you.
I'm so sorry, Jie...
Why apologize? You found the thing that I lost.
That which was lost has been restored. All is well.
I was negligent in my duty.
There's something I've always wanted to ask you, Jie.
You said you didn't want to use the writing knife to alter history, not even to change clerical errors. Why have you kept the knife around?
Did you forget that you already asked this question when you were three months on the job? You complained when I didn't allow you to use the writing knife to correct errors.
I-Is that so? I don't really remember...
Have you forgotten about your original aspiration, now that the erroneous descriptions of Yaan Elves have been addressed?
No, I just...

It's true that one should not pursue what lies in the past. But just as you and I have demonstrated, it does not mean that we should ignore the errors of the past, never mind trying to whitewash them.
I have kept the knife so that people will remember that wrongs can always be righted.
...I understand.
Nothing could be changed... tearing the pages out, writing over the words, they all do nothing because the page is instantly restored to its original state... how could this be?
How could this be?
This is her power.
She was not so difficult a hundred years ago, when it was proposed that she should leave the Imperial Historical Cabinet and refrain from writing the histories directly.

She did not complain, and indeed transferred to the Sky Mirror Pavilion of her own accord to work on reviews. She also reduced the number of courses she taught at the Academy, mostly working in an advisory capacity, similar to how Jun operates.
But her photographic memory, enthusiasm for history, and her immortality, were too much of a temptation to historians.
It became their habit to seek the approval of the one who is never wrong, and she is happy to take on the work, reviewing documents and histories...
Today, nothing that passes through her hands can be altered without her approval.
As for the writing knife that occupies so much of your mind... it was once an ordinary tool, when Jie first picked it up.
After spending centuries by Jie's side, it became capable of altering text without having to carve out bamboo.
Two centuries later, the writing knife accidentally erased a page from the veritable records of a True Lung.
Few, if any, ever perused those pages, and all who knew the subject were gone. And so, when a careless historian's hand slipped, no one could recall what the page held anymore.
One reason was that no copies were made of the veritable records. But it is also true that Jie's powers are growing.
If the Sui Regulator, or Jie herself, chooses to erase a word, a sentence, or perhaps a memory from under heaven with it... it is well within the realm of possibility.
The knife represents only a fragment of Jie's powers. How fast are her own powers growing?
The deeper Yan's history grows, the harder it will become to rein her in, for she is one with history.
In another century or ten, as she continues to immerse herself in history, she will become more powerful, more terrible, even than the bestial Sui Itself.
The long-lived Candleholder stands dumbfounded, hardly able to comprehend what she has just heard.
If that's all, I will return to the Sky Mirror Pavilion.
Very well. Be careful.
Wang...?
Are you alright?
I'm fine.
Wang, did you...?
Silence. They have grown used to this tacit understanding between them, in the long centuries that they have spent together.
Jie clearly sees the emotions in his eyes.
Flames of fury, well-hidden, but burning nonetheless.
It happened a century ago. I still remember the conflict that occurred because of the writing knife.
What is it that you want me to see, this time?

lines.st: I thought you would charge recklessly into the dream and tear it apart.
You call yourself a schemer. Did you not see how the seeds of your end were already sown?
The hearts of men are fickle...

lines.x: I don't need you to tell me.
So you did not interfere in my power struggle against the True Lung.

lines.st: Does it matter that you know? You dug your own grave. I did not need to do anything.
Besides, you have not seen the full picture of how the event led to her ultimate demise.
The end is near. Are you ready?

lines.loc: Two days before the day of the full moon, Hour of Hai

lines.x: The man walks past rows of fallen bookshelves, trying to return the scattered books to where they should be.

'The Earth's blessings have granted this stone to its people; the Heaven's blessings shall bring an end to Sui'... but in this thousand-year river of history, is it fate ordained by heaven and earth, or nothing more than a coincidence?
Lord Zuo and Miss Ch'en...
It was they who brought the token to the Sky Mirror Pavilion today...
Was it a coincidence, or...
This mayhem... what happened?
Someone joins him, stepping lightly, and bends down to pick up a book from the ground.
Mr. Liang.
Miss Ning... why are you still here?
Did you forget that I am an official of the Ministry of Rites? Neither of us should leave, until all the civilians have been evacuated.
You are quite right...
Is the evacuation of the civilians complete?
Essentially. Baizao will soon separate.
Tis unsettling... is it not?
Perhaps you wanted to ask how I knew you were here, when you did not tell me about your demotion.
...It rained through the night yesterday. I only fear that you may be cold...
The man clears a little spot for the two of them to sit down.
It has been a long time since I last visited the Sky Mirror Pavilion.
I suppose you used to frequent here?
As did all the students of the Academy.
I had a headache whenever I looked up at the books stacked all the way up to the ceiling.
But now... I find here a place of rare peace, away from the troubles beyond.
I... I heard about Mr. Ning.
You have my deepest sympathies....
My grandfather did what he felt was right.
He would not have kept the secret for so long out of selfish interests. He did it to protect the younger generations too.
I cannot reproach him, whether with reason or sentiment.
Of course, he must also pay the price for his deeds.
I see you have laid it down... I am glad to see it.
Perhaps you misunderstand. I do not mean that I could lay it down easily.
I cannot deny that my career benefited greatly from my grandfather's influence... those on the outside would undoubtedly say that the House of Ning has fallen on hard times.
I would not say this to anyone but you, Mr. Liang.
You should not say that. I do not feel that any of your honors are undeserved.
I trust you—
What if you are the only one?
My grandfather said that once one has passed the walls of the palace, one must not trust in others easily, and certainly not expect to be trusted easily.
I listened... but it seems that I forgot about it when I met you in Shangshu.
I simply believed that I found a kindred spirit.
You... you flatter me.
You've always been like this.
I admire your honesty and your sense of responsibility, but I hate how you make decisions by yourself and keep other people at a distance.
But then I realized... you would not be Liang Xun, if everything you did was to my liking.
I could no more reproach you than I could reproach my grandfather.
I once thought that all I needed to do was to be fair, honest, diligent, and persevering.
But the years have taught me how powerless I am.
Your grandfather was right, but even more frightening is the thought that I could not even trust myself to keep my aspirations and ideals.
I would not claim that everything I did was honest and upright... but you should not have been involved in the ones that were not.
Is it my turn to comfort you with the words 'clear conscience'?
You and I have done all we could, following the path of justice that we believed in.
Perhaps we are ultimately powerless to change anything. Perhaps this entire city will be no more when the sun rises tomorrow...
Then... here and now, at least... my conscience is clear.
The night is deep.
The ancient library has kept some old rules. All is quiet, except the passage of time.
They hold each other's hands tightly in their own.
The desolate pond lies wrinkled by the wind; beneath the crumbling eaves, no voices remain. One can still recall those days of green sashes and red plumes in their splendor...

lines.loc: One day before the day of the full moon, Hour of Tzu

lines.x: The scriptgeists seem to be appearing less frequently.
Could it be that you were right, and these geists are the breathing of the bestial Sui...?
I told you so. You just won't believe me.
Whatever the reason, this is our opportunity to head for the top—
Hm... so this is the Sky Mirror Pavilion's restricted area.
What are you planning?
These geists turn back into words when they're defeated.
Say, if they were transformed from the secret tomes of the Sky Mirror Pavilion, would defeating them reveal the secrets of Yan that are usually kept hidden?
You dare—?
No sudden moves!
Let me see what secrets the words form!
'Sang' 'Tu' 'Cui' 'Shou'... what is this? It makes no sense.
The top three floors of the Sky Mirror Pavilion are off-limits. We only enter because we have an important mission.
Refrain from doing anything unnecessary.
Hah. You still won't admit that you're already in on this.
Your deceptive words mean nothing. I know what I'm doing...
There's a lot that you don't know.
This building began as a public library, one of the first buildings to be completed after Baizao became a nomadic city. But there was no such thing as a restricted area at the time.
Then, one hundred and twenty years ago, the death of the proxy affected tons of history books, causing their text to become unreadable.
The Sui Regulator wanted to dig deep, but the number of books was enormous, so the Sky Mirror Pavilion set aside space up top for the Sui Regulator's research.
Decades passed. When the discussion of the turmoil had almost settled, the place became a warehouse for dusty old books.
Some restricted zone. Why would it be sealed off from public view, if it held any actual knowledge?
You do know a lot...
Just a little bit more than you.
The things you speak of should not be secrets to the Sui Regulator. But I have not heard them before.
My studies have been inadequate.
You—this is what I hate about you. Always stiff, always by the book.
Was that white-haired Perro we met in Mangshan Town a friend of yours?
You mean... Yun Qingping?
Yeah, that's the name, I think.
He couldn't fight his way out of a wet paper bag, and it looked like a stiff wind could blow him over, but he was a quick thinker. Talking to him was kind of interesting.
Why don't you learn a thing or two from him?
I know Yun's smart! I don't need you to lecture me!
Hahaha! Now you start to drop the official tone and sound like a human being.
...Why did you join the Shanhaizhong, Mo Yi?
What answer do you want to hear?
You want me to tell you that I had a difficult childhood, drifted into the Shanhaizhong, and I'm looking for a chance to repent and mend my ways?
Doesn't that sound a little cliche?
I wasn't—
Say whatever you want.
Of course, the Shanhaizhong who murder and pillage are villains. They deserve to die.
But I hate hypocrites even more, those people who look like they'll die if they stopped flaunting their high and mighty morals for a single moment.
They think they're better than everyone else the moment they come out of their mama's tummy. Frauds!
What I really want to know is... why are you so sure that this thing from the Garden of Grotesqueries will stop a war between Yan and Sui?
You've been with the Sui Regulator for a while. Have you never wondered about their most closely-guarded secret...?
Why did that proxy die?
Looks like it's not that you're not curious, it's that curiosity has yielded no answers.
That's the problem, isn't it? Why would this have become a secret, if the Sui Regulator was really so just and upright?
What's the matter?
No, it's nothing...
Did you not hear any of that?
You're so weird. You've been staring into blank space for a while now.
Did those geists give you a concussion? Don't suppose there's anything I can do about that.
Thanks. I'm not quite—
I just feel like...
I've been here before...
You said you've dreamt about this place before. Were you dreaming just now?
Perhaps.
I thought I heard two people arguing... something about 'justice', but I'm not sure...
You must be hearing things. It's mostly ancient history books here. Maybe there's a clue as to what's going on here, but justice? That's one thing you won't find.
They seemed to be saying that justice doesn't exist, tangibly...
That's true enough.
Never mind that. Let's get searching. Maybe you'll find your father's books here.
His books, here?
Talk about forgetting one's roots.
But I suppose it's not your fault. You didn't grow up in Baizao, and these things didn't really have anything to do with you.
I thought you would be angrier at me.
I am angry, but I realize I shouldn't take it out on you.
I just... never mind.
Miscellaneous Morsels from Baizao Suiyang... yes, this one dates back several generations.

'The people rejoiced as the new emperor took the throne. They put up white banners like clouds and held rites in remembrance of the previous emperor's virtue. But their hearts were hopeful, confident that an era of greatness was upon us, and the nation's peace secure...'
This is from a century ago, when the last True Lung took the throne.
That's a long time ago. Does it have anything to do with what we're investigating?
The last True Lung's accomplishments were innumerable. The transformation of Baizao into a nomadic city, as well as the Sky Mirror Pavilion, were completed under his decree.
Not that either of us were around back then... although I suppose he would be your grandfather.
Look at this foreword... talk about sucking up to the powerful.
The county records from around Baizao and the demographic records of Baizao itself all paint a picture that is far less rosy.
In the end, only one person's accomplishments remain.
Same old story no matter where you are, I guess.
That is kind of strange to hear from a close blood relative of that emperor.
'When the city of Baizao was completed, the emperor ordered the tomes of all the archives to be gathered in a single place.'
'The scholars worked by candlelight, collecting fragments and tales, validating the facts and compiling them, so that the scattered records are whole again.'
That's strange. The Imperial Historical Cabinet's efforts to collect the scattered histories happened after the death of the proxy, more than a decade before Baizao was built.
But the ambiguous way that it's written... the older generations were always stringent about scholarship. Why would they make such a mistake?
Could it be that... even what little they left behind was altered and rewritten?
Ch'en Hui-chieh is not listening to her companion's muttering. She is on guard for any unusual movement around them.
She wanted to tell Ch'en Chao-ch'ien to get moving, but she had a hard time saying it, seeing the sad look on Ch'en Chao-ch'ien's face.
I never asked you... what happened to your family? The ones who were in Baizao?

The Ch'ens were a family of historians. They were well-regarded but not illustrious. They generally stayed out of conflict and politics, other than the occasional offense they inevitably caused in the course of their work as historians.
All I remember is that my father said it was as though the sky had fallen when the family learned about the marriage arrangement with the princess.
That cousin was the only unmarried man in the family. He, and everyone else, knew how dangerous it was, but they were in no position to refuse...
