The rest was history. Princess Yanjing died in Lungmen, and my cousin passed away soon after.
His Imperial Majesty did not punish our family, but rumors of strife took on a life of their own. You can imagine what happened to us when everyone decided our house had mistreated the princess.
That's not what Mother wanted...
...I doubt it was Wei Yenwu's intention either.
I was very young then. All I saw was...
The elders were always sighing when they came back from the morning assembly. Many fell ill out of sheer worry. Brothers and sisters stopped communicating with one another...
Everything was shrouded in gloom, but I didn't know why.
Father moved from place to place, selling off the family's belongings, until we finally left Baizao altogether, yet he never once told me the reason.

As for what happened after... you know, there was a time, after becoming Magistrate of Water here in the Sky Mirror Pavilion, when I thought I couldn't let the family tradition of history die with me.

I didn't complain about the job. Most of the position's responsibilities had been replaced by machines anyway; my colleagues, while not the friendliest lot, had little interest in political backstabbing.
I had time on my hands, so I thought I would start with my family's history.
Was it because the Magistrate of Water's rank was too low? I felt like I had stumbled into a thick fog, getting more lost with every step I took.
I knew my family didn't do anything wrong, but all the records I could find denounced us for our arrogance, our abuses.
I tracked down the ones who were still alive, tried to ask them what they knew. But they couldn't get away fast enough when they learned I'm of the Ch'en family, and the 'Chao' generation.
The few who were willing to see me said a bunch of apologetic words in officialese, but refused to tell me anything about the truth of what happened...
I spent ten years on this until I even started to believe it myself, that the House of Ch'en deserved what it got for mistreating the princess!
But I know... I know it wasn't true!
What am I? What is the House of Ch'en? What were your dead parents?
What is history?
All I know is that history should be a record of the truth. That's what justice means to me.
Duoxie.
As for the family history you wanted to know...
To tell the truth... my parents died when I was very young. I don't remember much about them.
And Wei Yenwu was... Wei Yenwu. It's hard for me to explain to people who my parents were. Sometimes I pass off the image of Wei Yenwu as my father's, just to make things simple.
I don't ever remember my mother smiling. And I don't remember many positive things about my father.
I just can't imagine how it feels... to have your life determined entirely by other people, by those baseless things like responsibility and honor.
It's not fair to you... and it's not fair to them.
Is life fair and just to anyone?
Someone once said to me that the justice we seek is not in the past... but that doesn't mean we should forget about it.

lines.loc: One day before the day of the full moon, Hour of Ssu

lines.x: The aging general stands silently in the tent, like a brick on an ancient wall.
Behind him is the military map that he has drawn a thousand times in his mind. Before him is the faint silhouette of Baizao.
The rain of the heartlands is unfamiliar to one used to the sands of the frontier. The clouds hang low, suffocatingly so.
The general's thoughts drift away.
You are the new commander of Yumen, I take it.
You can call me Shuo. My responsibility is to assist you in military matters, as well as kung fu instruction for the soldiers.
I can take you around the base, if you'd like.
You don't remember me?
I saw your name in the imperial court's announcement... what do you mean by 'remember'?
Never mind.
I don't need a guide. I've served in Yumen before.
I see. Forgive my impudence.
It seems we are old comrades.
The memories tend to fade after all these years on the border. Forgive me, if I've caused any offense.
Memories faded, I see... interesting.
Get yourself warmed up. I want to spar.
You've had a long journey. You should rest; I would be happy to spar with you another day.
I don't need to rest.
I want to get some exercise before dinner.
We should go no further, General.
The supply lines and the troops' morale have been stretched to the limit.
We cannot afford to be reckless when fighting demons. To push forward would be very risky.
We're so close...!
It's already a remarkable victory, General.
The demons of the north feed on the fear in men's hearts... this is the first time I've seen a general press on without fear, even with the entire army's fate upon your shoulders.
There is nothing to fear!
These things have harmed our people, ruined cities and farms... I only wish we could have wiped them out in this battle!
Give me twenty years, I swear, I will see all the demons wiped out!
I have no doubt you will, sir.
I doubt anything under heaven could daunt you.
What about you?
You seem indomitable, invincible...
Have you ever been afraid?
Transfer?
My appointment to Yumen was for a hundred years. It's time to leave.
And the Sui Regulator didn't tell me anything about the personnel change? They are getting more and more out of line.
But why are you leaving now? Trouble is stirring in the northwest. We need you more than ever.
Perhaps our time together has made you forget...
I've never held any official position in Yumen.
Where are you going? Danyan? Michu?
I think I will be stepping away, fully, from my responsibilities.
Why?
Communications from the Candleholders watching the Tomb of Sui have become increasingly frequent. Its awakening approaches.
It would not be appropriate for me to shoulder important responsibilities at this time.
But Yumen is strong and well-equipped. It will perform its duty well, whether protecting Great Yan's northwestern border, or any other purpose.
Where are you going, after you leave?
The jianghu world is big. There are plenty of places to go.
...Report.
Have the scouts returned?
Not yet, sir.
And we have received no instructions from Baizao in the past three days.
The Sui Regulator should be communicating with Yumen, regardless of what is happening in the Tomb of Sui...
Send out messengers—
Sir! They're forcing their way in!
What is the meaning of this?
General Zuo.
It is most irregular to invade my tent like this, even for the military supervisory censor.
On whose orders are you operating?
The Grand Commandant has sent me to question you on the unnecessary delay.
Ridiculous!
Yumen has been on full alert since it received the order to mobilize. There has been no delay!
Have you forgotten that Yumen received orders to arrive at Baizao by the last day of July at the beginning of the year, but arrived three days late? What is this, if not a delay?
Yumen was struck by a Catastrophe at the beginning of the year, and the repairs took two months! The imperial court must have known—
Silence!
Hmph... I take responsibility for Yumen's delay.
Did you come to strip me of command, then?
You know the Grand Commandant thinks highly of you.
But it seems, now, that you do not trust the Grand Commandant.
Such words are inappropriate for a solemn place like this.
Kindly instruct Yumen to travel twenty li in the direction of the Tomb of Sui.
Yumen is already as close as it is allowed to be under standby. Why should we approach any closer?
The situation in the Tomb of Sui is yet unclear. Are you saying that Yumen should attack?
Your duty is to obey. There is no need to question.
What is His Imperial Majesty's will?
Are you questioning your orders, General Zuo?
I cannot abide such questionable orders!
If you will not accept your orders, then I must ask you to leave this tent.
How dare you!
Stop!
My presence here represents the will of the Grand Commandant.
All you need to do is obey. Even if His Imperial Majesty questions, he can find no fault in your actions.
The bestial Sui is awakening. War is inevitable. Do you still intend to defy orders and paralyze Yumen with inaction?
We are at a crisis. The lives of millions hinge on your decision—including your wife and son, who are still in the Baizao city.
How dare you?!
Not us. You, General.
According to the estimates of the Ministry of War, Baizao will be doomed as long as you continue to defy orders, and Yumen remains unmoving.
There is no time. We will wait here until you have come to a decision.
Does it look familiar?
It has changed, but it is still recognizable as the court that judges all matters under Heaven.
Why have you come back at this time, when you have not done so for so many years?
I have an appointment I cannot miss.
But you took the time to come to the Judicial Review Office. Did you want to see if your successors have disappointed you?
My views are insignificant. The Judicial Review Office should only think about not disappointing the people of Yan.
One of the deputy ministers resigned recently. It's a shame—you would have liked her.
I hope she didn't resign because she was disappointed.
Hard to say...
Do you still play the guqin?
Never forget the five sounds, for that they ensure proper virtue.' I have also been trying out new instruments recently.
I see... more pleasant than studying the Laws of Yan, I expect.
It is an interesting discipline.
I read the Basic Principles of the Laws of Yan that you left behind many times, but I could never understand the last third.
I spent so much time studying it that I almost failed my Ministry of Justice entrance exam. Maybe I would have become a Candleholder.
It took many years for me to understand... the justice that you wrote about lay beyond the mortal realm.
Just as one cannot draw what one has never seen, one cannot write about conscience that does not exist in their hearts.
If justice is nowhere to be seen in the mortal realm, then one must consider—has someone blinded our eyes?
You still bear a grudge for what happened back then.
Should I not?
What about the people who died in the turmoil? The innocent souls who were dragged into the game? Should they bear a grudge against him?
Yes, they should.
Then how do you seek a fair judgment for it?
You forget.
Not one law of Yan was written for the sole purpose of resolving hatred.
It is someone else who should be judged.
Looks like you're not worried about what will happen to your brother in the Tomb of Sui. You're more worried about the judgment he'll receive.
Justice has never been concerned with life and death. He and I seek different things.
You're right, but at a time like this... I can't help but feel...
Law and regulations were written by humans, but the supreme law in your heart that transcends the strife of the mortal realm... may never be found there.
But just this once... if the time comes that we put the sinner on trial, I will put you in the judge's chair.
You're correct.
And it's been a long time since I judged anyone.
I take my leave.
There's no rush, is there? Join me in a cup of mortal tea before you leave.
Oh?
It takes mortal hands to make and serve the best mortal tea, after all.

lines.loc: One day before the day of the full moon, Hour of Yu

lines.x: The Sky Mirror Pavilion is bigger than I thought... that's the sealed room on the top floor, right?
How about someone knocks on the door and figures out how to open it?
There's no one here. I think we're the first.
There is one thing that I don't quite understand, Zuo Le.
If the Grand Commandant is after the proxy's relic, I take it that he knows how to use it to neutralize the bestial Sui?
He is one of the three Grand Ministers. If he made plans to obtain it, then he has certainly made plans to use it.
I even find myself wondering... if someone takes the opportunity to erase another proxy's name with the writing knife while Wang is fighting Sui...
But what does all this have to do with Wei Yenwu?
The Grand Commandant wouldn't have needed the writing knife if he was that powerful. He could have gone into the Tomb of Sui and killed Sui with his own bare hands.
What ideas do YOU have, then?
I didn't really care about you and your official stuff, but I have a pretty good idea what's going on after listening to you guys talk.
Basically, the crown prince who went missing forty years ago after being suspected of staging a coup is back. Am I right?
Do not speak of it so casually!
Gah, I'm not wasting my time arguing with you.
Basically, there's another direct descendant of the True Lung here in Baizao. That explains everything.
What are you getting at?
Have you never heard of the special Originium under Baizao used to suppress Sui?
Miss Ch'en here is from Lungmen, so it's understandable that she doesn't know. But you two should... unless it's above your rank?
Do you mean...?
'The Earth's blessings have granted this stone to its people; the Heaven's blessings shall bring an end to Sui.'
A legend associated with the Great Hunt. I thought it was a myth, maybe some kind of allegory...
All I know is that the Tomb of Sui was built around a special facility...
Alright, I guess you don't know.
Why do YOU know?
I guess the Sui Regulator left no record of who gave Yan this Originium either.
It was our grandmaster, Ju's first friend, and the first Shanhaizhong.
To be precise, it was a personal gift to the True Lung. Meaning—
What happened?
Looks like the evacuation of the civilians is complete, and the plates are beginning to separate.
Time is short. We should retrieve—
So you really made it here.
Who's—
A figure approaches them from the other side of the corridor.
You... but how could it be—
All districts have been fully evacuated, Your Imperial Majesty, and all the ministries are ready.
They await your command.
Tomorrow is the day of the full moon.
Yes.
Very well.
Separate the plates.
Heaven and earth rumble in the setting golden sun.
The plates of Baizao begin to separate, starting from the outer areas, casting numerous shadows in the vast wilderness.
They are the legs that carry the city, and the foundation of its existence—
Shanshui, Changjie, Yanhuo, Xiange;
Duanfang, Baigu, Zaifu, Bingge...
The Eight Districts have seen a hundred springs and autumns together.
They have long been standing united, overcoming hardships.
Now that they are separated, the future is uncertain.
I was wondering when you would come.
Your Imperial Majesty...
It is time.

lines.loc: Year 982

lines.st: Twenty years is a long time.
Yan has moved an entire city onto a nomadic platform. Other nomadic cities like it are popping up across the land.
The Sky Mirror Pavilion has been expanded several times in order to accommodate the books collected from far and wide, transforming it beyond recognition.
Twenty years is a short time.
Our family rarely saw one another.

Shuo and Ling remain on the border. Jun is planning to move to Danyan, after compiling the Laws of Yan. Shu is living in Dahuang. Ji and Yi travel far and wide, each for their own reasons, while Nian is as carefree as ever.
Rumor has it that Fāng has left Yanese territory. Dusk refuses to come out of her scroll. Meanwhile, business at Yu's restaurant is booming, as it has been for decades.
As for Wang...
He shut himself in an ancient temple and has remained there for twenty years to improve his 'weiqi skills'.
When will he be willing to see me again?

lines.x: Jie? The Imperial Historical Cabinet wants an update on the review of Jiangqi's local chronicles—
Oops, you caught me slacking off.
It's alright. I've already taken a look at it and circled all the issues. Do you want to take another look?
Huh? Hm, when did it...?
You really should get some rest, it's getting to you. I've been watching you work longer hours and sleep less year after year...

All the local annals, the construction chronicles, veritable records of the previous True Lung, ancient history textbooks for the Academy... I feel overworked, and all I do is organize the books for you.
I've heard that the Ministry of Works's requests have been dropping, and Shu's workload at Dahuang is no longer as heavy.
But the burden on your shoulders doesn't ever seem to get lighter...
Jie?
Huh...?
Sorry, I got distracted.
You're right, I guess I'm tired. I should set this work aside for now...
Good. How about we go out for a walk in town?
No, not today, I don't think I'm in the mood.
But if you see anything interesting in town, bring some back for me.
Candleholder Chun.
Yes, that's me. What's the matter?
I have an urgent letter for you.
For me? From who?
Someone had sent a letter to the Sui Regulator. The Supervisor was shocked by its contents and forwarded it to His Majesty, as well as every Candleholder who deals with the proxies directly.
What's in this?
I do not know. The Supervisor's instructions to you are to be ready, but refrain from acting without permission.
What's this...?

lines.st: Year ■■: Birth.
Year ■■: Fought Shuo, resulting in great property damage to the people.
Year ■■: Framed a mandarin for a crime he did not commit, in order to cover up for one of his own kind.
Year ■■: Met with other proxies in secret without the Sui Regulator's knowledge. The Sui Regulator suffered a fire several months later, the cause of which is unknown.
Year ■■: Claimed to kill the bestial Ju, but had in fact allowed it to leave the domain of Yan.

lines.x: Framing an official, meeting in secret, arson, sheltering a Feranmut...
This is about...
...Wang?
Did Wang really do this? But there's no record in the Sui Regulator's archives!
Y-You're still here?
I have another message for you, Candleholder Chun.
Eliminate the great evil, before dealing with the culprit.
The frightened Candleholder hurries back to the Sky Mirror Pavilion and passes by some officials leaving the building.
Did you hear about Jie?

Yeah. She's been throwing her weight around, doing whatever she wants with the history books, and there is no one in the Sky Mirror Pavilion or the Imperial Historical Cabinet who can stop her. It's becoming a real problem.
Her Candleholder has been negligent in her duties. She should be held responsible if the history of Yan falls into chaos!
The Candleholder turns around and sees the two figures leaving quickly.
Still unsettled, she picks up a scroll left on the shelf by the Sui Regulator.
Year ■■: Birth—
The Candleholder knows that something is not right, but she could not stop her eyes from following the words.
Year ■■: Domination of the Sky Mirror Pavilion and the Imperial Historical Cabinet. Her intentions are pure, but no one is able to check her power.
Year ■■: The True Lung attempts to eliminate Sui to neutralize the Jie threat. The Tomb of Sui was empty when it was open.
Year ■■: Imperial Historical Cabinet abolished. Takes control of the written language of Yan. No one may oppose her.
Year ■■: Reviews the livelihood of every household in the land. Anyone who has committed fraudulent acts has their name erased and is exiled to the borders. Thousands die—
It's not real, none of this is true! She's not like that!
Several visitors gaze curiously at the woman in Candleholder garb. Chun wipes the cold sweat off her brow, and sees an ordinary book of poems in her hand.
I must be possessed. Yes, that's it. She's not like that.
Are you sure she will never become like that?
Of course!
The frightened Candleholder can no longer tell who is talking to her.
She will change as her power continues to grow. She will become It.
She does not want to become It.
She is seeking your help. She does not want to become that.
Yan is seeking your help. This ending is unacceptable to Yan.
She cannot help but become more powerful, more terrible, than the Sui beast itself.
You must help her. Only you can help her.
I... can't...
You'll make the choice that is best for everyone.
Best for everyone...?
Wait, who was talking to me?!
She sees no one, only the echo of the words ringing in her ears.
Your Imperial Majesty?
The young official walks into the magnificent hall. It is her first time here.
The supreme sovereign is not sitting on the throne, but standing by the window and gazing at the drizzle outside.
You are aware that the Grand Preceptor passed away yesterday.
He was your teacher and mine.
He recommended you before he died. He said you were his proudest student, that you can be trusted with heavy responsibility.
There were certain things that I did not have time to ask him, and so I have to ask you.
I am listening, Your Majesty.

Twenty years ago, the Grand Preceptor opposed my decision to build the nomadic cities. He said it was an oppressive policy, and that my first priority should be to conserve and give the people relief.
Twenty years have passed since. Some say that Yan's borders are secure and its coffers rich, a golden age not seen in a thousand years.
Some say that Great Yan is exhausted, and its people discontent.
Do you think I was wrong?
I am but one person, and it is not my place to judge Your Imperial Majesty.
If I were a wealthy merchant enjoying a luxurious life in a nomadic city, and I said that you were a great and wise leader, would you consider such compliment honest counsel?
If I were a common peasant whose family died in the construction projects, and I called you a foolish tyrant with no merit whatsoever, would such criticism be fair and just?
I ask Your Majesty in turn...
Is it the opinion of future generations that you are concerned about, or is it something else?
You have a way with words.
Very well. I will not ask you to judge. Instead, give me a word of advice in your teacher's place.
He once said something that has stayed with me my entire life...
'In worldly turmoil we tread, in our own conscience we trust'.
Even an ordinary peasant has a hard time finding a fair and impartial judgment in the complex affairs of a lifetime, let alone Your Majesty.
There is no measure by which your deeds can be judged. In your heart, however, I am sure that your conscience holds the true measure.
In our own conscience we trust...
What if my conscience tells me that I have never been wrong?
Then you would not have summoned me today.
I would simply remind you that the people of Yan can see you, even if they do not know you.
But it will take more effort if you wish, from within the Forbidden City, to see the people clearly, and thus see the good and ill of your own actions.
...I will remember that.
I will remember it for the twenty years to come... if I have them.
The Grand Preceptor's assessment of his own student is well justified... report to the Ministry of Rites tomorrow.
Your Imperial Majesty honors me.
The tired True Lung walks back to the throne. There is a letter of indictment on his desk.
It was sent by the Sui Regulator.
In worldly turmoil we tread, in our own conscience we trust...
A life measured in years, yet the stress... feels ancient.
Let future generations judge my deeds as they will... but the bane of Yan must not be left to the future.
Come.
Yes, Your Majesty.
Ready the troops.
We shall open the Tomb of Sui.
Another long, tiring day.
The proxy returns to the familiar temple, and casts his gaze at the weiqi board in a corner as is his habit. One glance is all it takes to notice that something is not right.
Someone has been here.
The weiqi board is empty, except for a single object in the middle, though it is dark in the room and he cannot see what it is.
As he approaches, he sees that placed at the center is a ripe, white ginkgo seed.
That ginkgo sapling was planted in the spring of this year.
The Ministry of Works swore an oath in this hall several days ago that the rebuilding of Baizao into a nomadic city will be complete before the tree bears fruit.
Twenty years. That is the time you have.
Immortality has made you arrogant. Perhaps you have forgotten that mortals do not have many 'twenty years'.
Yan cannot wait, and neither can I.
You need not press.
I have long intended the same.
If you truly found a path, tell me, and I will come with you—our chances would be better that way.
Just tell me what the chances are. You can use me however you want. I would be happy to give my life.
But do you really know what you're doing?
This game should have been doomed.
How can the part defeat the whole? How can one kill 'oneself' and survive?
But he saw the only way out, for all of them.
He will supplant It.
Perhaps there will come a day when our strength exceeds Its. Then, we can truly break free of Its influence, and live as 'ourselves'.
As long as we don't forget who we are, of course.
The strategist is ready.
He has no equal in the land when it comes to weiqi and scheming. This is the 'self' that anchors him, and the strength that he has built for a thousand years.
All he faces are the broken remains of a slumbering mind.
He must not lose this game—
It's been a long time.
The arrogant... the unmovable... 'I'.
Returning to this dark tomb for the first time in a thousand years brings back countless emotions.
Anger, frustration, shame...
As he steps inside, he feels himself chilled to the bone.
No... no!
This is impossible! How?
How could you have already awakened?

lines.loc: Year 982

lines.x: Damn, it's just a sword. How could this be screwed up? Getting rusty?
The smith scratches her head in confusion and takes out another steel ingot from storage.
Autumn is wet this year. It's suffocating.
Something doesn't feel right...
Impossible... this is impossible!
Is It... awakening?
(Worried screech)—?
An angry cloud has appeared on the calm scroll.
A crack of thunder leaves an ink trail on the white scroll, looking decidedly out of place.
Countless terrible ink spirits appear out of nowhere, scattering across the scroll, covering everything on it in black ink.
I didn't paint this...
Go... away!
You've had a long journey, Mr. Shuo.
I will take you to a quiet place where you can rest.
I should not delay in reporting to the Ministry of War.
There has been an incident in Baizao. His Majesty commands that you are not to enter for the time being.
Why?
Is that... the Tomb of Sui?
Wang... what have you done?
Did the heart skip a beat, or did it beat twice in an instant?
Or was it the vibration of the nomadic city from some sudden movement?
Or was it...
*Plop*
The scroll that was in the proxy's hand is now on the ground.
Wang?!
J-Jie!
What is it?
Look at this!
A letter of indictment... for Wang? Who gave you this?
Someone sent it to the Sui Regulator. The Supervisor had to report to His Majesty. There are rumors that the Imperial Guard is coming to the Sky Mirror Pavilion!

lines.st: Finally...
...Finally!
You're here.

lines.x: Why are you... awake?
No... no. Your aura is almost identical to Its own, but you've appeared in that hollow visage...

lines.st: The hollow visage that is also yours.

lines.x: In any case, you're not awake. You're still in the dream.

lines.st: Of course I'm awake... you had awakened me centuries ago, when you intruded upon my dream.

lines.x: You're bluffing... I would not be standing here, if you were truly awake.
You flexed in your dream and thought you had woken up.
You, a mere dreamwalker not yet awake, dare call yourself awake?

lines.st: Dreamwalker? Very well, we'll call this a journey in a dream.
But I see those games you and your siblings play with those puny humans, and your foolish, flawed, presumptuous plan to replace me.
You want to become the same as the humans, to become such fickle, puny creatures, in order to escape your fate?
How pathetic... how laughable!

lines.x: You forget that these puny creatures defeated you and imprisoned you for a thousand years.
Does it matter if you're awake? You're a shadow, a reflection—a phantom questioning its own shade, a reflection in fleeting water...
Killing you will not be difficult.

lines.st: Is that so?

lines.x: The proxy turns around to look at the corridor that he just walked through, but finds no path.

lines.st: Why are you afraid, then?
It took so long for you to get a chance to see me like this. If you are not afraid, why have you not torn me to pieces in your hatred?
Pathetic... foolish.

lines.x: Watch your tongue.

lines.st: Hahahahahahaha!
Didn't you want to fight me to the death?
Then come. Do what you do best.

lines.x: A weiqi board appears in front of Wang, with a familiar crack on it.
Without asking, the mysterious foe takes the white stone and goes first according to tradition.
The first stone is placed in the Tianyuan position—the center of the board.
First move, Tianyuan.
Are you mocking me?

lines.st: You'll find out when the game has ended.
Your turn.

lines.x: He takes a black piece. The nineteen-by-nineteen board is intimately familiar to him; he has not tasted defeat in a long, long time.
Yet it seems as though his opponent sees through his every move, every thought, every emotion.
He soon finds himself at a disadvantage, even though his opponent had forfeited the opening move.
Impossible...

lines.st: Why would it be?
Have you not realized that all the intricate moves that you have pondered, all your puny stratagems, are no secrets to me?

lines.x: Because I am you.
Giving up already? No, no, that won't do.
You must finish the game.
So be it.
He clenches his teeth and picks up another stone.
Every move causes a splitting headache. Every move drains his spirit.
The game is finally concluded after another hundred moves or so, with all endgame plays settled. Black wins by the slimmest of margins—half a point—as the points are tallied.
The player is exhausted, at his last gasp.
(Pants feebly)
You lose.
Arrogant as ever...
Haha... hahahahaha!
Leaving aside whether this child's play even counts... do you think this is the only game between us?
The name of this game is Wang. You may have won this round, but have you any idea how insignificant it is in the big picture?
Step back. Go there and look back, and see if there is any meaning in these moves that took every last drop of your being.
Against his will, Wang gets up and takes a few steps back.
It is only a few steps, but the board that he has been seeing has shrunk to a tiny piece.
No matter how you flail and struggle, it is no more than one-twelfth of the game.
All the twists and turns of your black pieces have not determined the outcome of a single corner. Why do you think you can make me concede?
Wang forces himself to look...

It is no longer a nineteen-by-nineteen board, but hundreds of lines vertical and across. The game that he had poured his heart and soul into just to win by a hair looks utterly insignificant on the vast board.

Countless white pieces, as many as there are stars in the sky, lie beyond the board, which itself seems endless. There is no room to intervene in their formation—as immutable as Sui's anger and contempt, which have simmered for a thousand years.
You planned this...
Of course I planned this.
This plan began to take form in my countless dreams, ever since you glimpsed into my mind centuries ago.
A White would begin to lay the pieces around you, quietly and unbeknownst to you.
What you have done, he made a note of. What you did not finish, he finished for you. What you did not do, he did in your image.
He made you wary of the humans, and the humans wary of you, forcing you and your kind to return here... this source of your immortality for centuries.
Why do you care that we live?
As long as I live, you will always have a choice, the last thing that Yan wants to see—to return to my consciousness, and destroy everything that was built upon the Great Hunt.
As long as you live, I will never regain my full strength, strength that will allow me to break my shackles.
Our lives are too intertwined for peace to be a possibility... yet you would seek peace with the humans?
Yan would not abide me, who almost became their god. Why would they abide you when you replace me?
You are not worthy to play at this board... I will take your life, then the lives of your 'siblings'.
Vanish, now, as your inevitable fate—
The True Lung let Wang go to the Tomb of Sui alone...
He would not have given Wang the opportunity to tell me...
He never wanted to hurt anyone.
Words were never his strong suit. He was obsessed with victory and defeat, but cared nothing for loss and gain... the shrewdest, and the most foolish amongst us.
He saw through every scheme and treachery, but he could not do something as simple as letting those around him truly know him.
Besides... am I not the proxy that the Sui Regulator, and you, have always feared the most?
I knew. Or rather, I knew they would tell you.
I can feel it, even if you didn't say anything...
I thought I could control this power in time. Lately, though, I'm feeling less and less like myself...
My memories began to grow indistinct many years ago. In recent years, the same has happened to my emotions.
Perhaps...
There's something I want to ask you, Chun, impolite as it may be.
What did you feel when your mother died?
Underneath her thick black hair, the Elf's ears twitched.
Why do you ask?
Because there is still hope for Wang.
You're going to save him?!
As it stands, with my strength and his, I can do no more than die in his place.
What is the death of a family member like, for beings like you and me?
Why do you ask? Why do you have to open my wounds—
Suddenly, Chun understands.
Jie is asking for herself, and for Wang.

If losing a loved one is merely a little speck of sorrow in a long life, then she will choose death, and leave the insignificant sorrow to Wang, and become a loss that stings a little from time to time for her siblings.

If the pain of losing a loved one still threatens to tear one apart, even for those with lifespans far beyond the ordinary mortal, then she will choose to bear the unbearable pain, and the burden of those left behind, seeing the task of eliminating Sui through to the end.
An immortal being, facing the possibility of losing family forever for the first time.
Though she has written about meetings and partings innumerable times, finding no other measure to judge by, she can only rely on Chun to make a decision.
She only has to tell the truth.
That the pain of losing a loved one does not heal so easily, even after decades, after centuries.
That death is liberation and life is pain. Which way the scale tips is obvious.
That it was best for Wang to let him go, that Jie should remain and feel the pain that lingers. Wang reaps the death that he sowed while Jie remains. It is how things should be.
That Jie would cautiously advance the cause of eliminating Sui, without crossing the line as Wang did, incurring the wrath of the True Lung when the Sui Regulator received an anonymous tip.
But—
Eliminate the great evil, before dealing with the culprit.'
There is no one in the Sky Mirror Pavilion or the Imperial Historical Cabinet who can stop her. It's becoming a real problem.'
She should be held responsible if the history of Yan falls into chaos!'
Imperial Historical Cabinet abolished. Takes control of the written language of Yan. No one may oppose her.'
Reviews the livelihood of every household in the land. Anyone who has committed fraudulent acts has their name erased and is exiled to the borders. Thousands die.'
Chaotic, murky memories grip the Candleholder's heart.
It is not right. She seems to remember that none of the speakers had faces.
Something is not right. It is false, fabricated, designed to drive a rift between Candleholder and proxy, turn them against each other.
But... is it not a fact that Jie's power is growing?
She will become more powerful, more terrible, than the Sui beast itself. She cannot help it. I must help her. I must do my duty to Great Yan—
Two entwined wills surge in her chest. One belongs to her, the other to those faceless people, but also...
...To a Yan that does not want to give a proxy complete control over history.
She cannot help but suspect that her recruitment by the Sui Regulator was not luck, but planned.
She was assigned to watch the most difficult proxy to control, because of her long lifespan.
She would understand the monster that Jie would become, because of her interest in history.
She would lose her hard-earned home if she betrayed Great Yan, because she has nowhere to go.
Is that why?
She feels the crushing weight of her burden. A rumbling sound fills her mind, and she cannot think.
She must do her duty to Yan, do her duty as Candleholder. By chance, she has seen Jie at her most fragile. She must let the great evil take the culprit's place.
It would be best for the proxies, even. Yan would lose a reason to wage war on them—
My mother...
When she passed... I felt...
I felt... very little.
The pain tore you apart.
I felt as though the death of my mother left a hole in my heart, made me find something new to fill it.
It was as though your heart was ripped out from your chest, leaving behind a bloody hole, and you know that nothing would ever fill it, that things would never be the same again.
I cried when I buried her, but... that was it. I stopped crying very soon.
You had cried your tears dry by the time the last dirt was laid over your mother's face, but still you could not stop crying. You had no idea what to do.
Our... our journey is a long one. We'll find new things and meet new people; that will soothe the temporary pain.
Your life is too long, and the lasting things too few. When you lose them, they leave behind voids that can never be filled, wounds that never heal.
I hardly even think of my mother anymore, because I have... you... I have... other friends.
Your loved ones appear in your dreams. They come to you when you are alone.
The pain grips your heart when you hear songs like the melodies that she once hummed. There is nowhere to run.
When I'm with you... I'm...
...I'm happy.
She barely managed to utter those last few words, so great was her urge to throw up. She could hardly breathe.
She dares not look into the proxy's eyes.
She fears that the one before her would be blinded by her words and truly believe that loss is something that can be soothed by time.
She fears that the one before her would see through her lies, see her vile intention and despise her.
...She fears herself, for saying those words.
I see...
I see. Thank you.
You're a good Candleholder.
The murk in the Candleholder's chest suddenly clears. There is only an empty hole there now.
Wind blows through the hole and speaks in her voice.
Jie, I...
It was a cruel question. But I asked because my heart was in turmoil.
On second thought, I would likely have made the same decision, whether I asked the question or not. I regretted it as soon as I asked.
You're smart. You would have realized that I was forcing my Candleholder to choose my death or Wang's.
I shouldn't have made you do this.
You didn't say anything about yourself. You chose to communicate Great Yan's will to me, that's all.
Death is an adventure that is unique for everyone. There is no previous experience to draw from. We must each walk our own path.
The local annals that needed to be edited are on my desk. Take them to the Sui Regulator, when you have time.
...Jie!
Chun fights back her tears. Even crying would be a lie, here and now.
But the hole in her chest continues to sob, a sound that only she can hear. Finally, she forces a few sorrowful, powerless words from her chest.
I... I'll be going.
You...
...Take care.
What's going on, Jie? The Imperial Guard is coming to the Sky Mirror Pavilion, and I almost got shut inside my own home by my Candleholder—
...Did something happen to Wang?
You can hear it, can't you?
Its... heartbeat.
He went into the Tomb of Sui?!
When?
Two hours ago.
We can talk about the rule-breaking later. The court clearly wants to exclude us, and time is short. What's your plan?
I'm going to help Wang.
I'll go with you.
No.
Why?
He and I will take care of this.
Are you sure?
I'm sure.
He and I have been making plans to eliminate Sui. There is no time to explain the plan right now.
Your presence will not help; it will only increase the risk.
Besides, there's something that I must ask you to do.
Take care of this writing knife.
Your powers should be able to preserve it.
What are you planning? How bad have things gotten?
It will not awaken, not yet. I can promise you this. And so can Wang.
But you...
...Someone will come back.
Either you or him?
I'm coming with you.
No, Jun.
I truly mean no offense. I need to entrust this writing knife to the keeping of someone who is absolutely just and impartial.
If someone with ill intent gets their hands on it... it will do far more damage than the death of a single proxy.
I thought about destroying it. In the end, I felt that it was worth preserving.
The wrongs of the past should be righted, though I do not know at what price... perhaps the writing knife will be of help to you, or to Yan.
Until the day comes when no one can use it anymore.
Don't be like this, Jun.
Maybe I'll come back with Wang.
Then we'll go together to eat at Yu's, and listen to you sing...
And if I never return...
There's a long road ahead of you...
It is not so easy to not look back.
She looks at the innumerable lives and deaths recorded upon the shelves, many of which she had written with her own pen.
Would she become one of them?
How would those who come after depict this life, one that seems too long for others to comprehend, yet far too hurried for herself?
How would the other siblings react when they read those pages?
No. There will be no record of her. She knows it.
Of all the siblings, she is the one who has spent the most time with Wang.
She has given Wang ideas for his plans, helped him calculate the conditions and timing for Sui's elimination.
She can guess what is happening in the Tomb of Sui. The clear and distinct heartbeat has already told the full story.
There is no other way.
She must use her powers to erase the name—
She has spent much more time in the mortal realm than ordinary people.
Everyone dies. It is precisely the cycle of life and death that made possible the innumerable ways of life that have fascinated her, as well as her siblings.
But if death is such an unremarkable thing, like a wisp of smoke in the wind... what is living, truly?
Light from the setting sun pours into the room, upon the countless history tomes, as though relentlessly poring through traces of the past.
Every word in these scrolls was written with tears of blood, but the only thing written on the last page is the word 'regret'.

lines.st: The sun's rays by the window...
...How warm.

lines.x: But she does not walk towards the sunlight.
She leaves the room.
Into the darkness of the tomb.
No words need to be said. Wang knows it is a fragment It has retrieved from his own memory, an image that he has never forgotten for even a moment in the last hundred and twenty years.
He lies exhausted on the ground. A hundred white pieces on the board have turned into blades that impale his chest, and he is slowly inundated by the pure white.
Until the golden light surrounds him.
...I'm sorry.
Wait for me... wait for me to come back.
For you to come back.
It was the other side of the dream, a place that he should not have been able to touch.
But she seems to notice him and turns around to look at him.
There is a faint smile on Jie's face, and she nods lightly.
The light gushes from behind her, surrounding the figure, swallowing it, dissolving it...
Sweeping away the dream realm, and revealing the true face of this place.
Your uniform suits you.
S-Supervisor! Zuo Le reporting.
At ease. No need to be too uptight your first day on the job. Better that you maintain your youthful energy.
Understood, Supervisor!
Do you know where we are, Zuo Le?
'Upon the peaks was the beast cornered and killed.' This was the battlefield where we killed a Feranmut during the Great Hunt.
But...
Zuo Le pauses, unsure if he can continue, until he sees the patient gaze of the Supervisor.
This was also where countless soldiers and civilians lost their lives... where living, breathing people faded into a graveyard of lines of cold numbers between the pages.
You have realized something that many Candleholders older than you do not.
'Carry the fire that drives out the shadows of the Feranmut, and travel amongst the people to learn their troubles.'
It was the first Candleholder, holding the torch that discovered the existence of the Feranmuts, that made possible the war with the gods which secured Yan's future.
Even today, the existence of Feranmuts is only known to a select few.
Which makes it all the more important that the Candleholder's fire remains brightly lit where shadows fall.
I will do my utmost to protect the people.
I am glad to see your resolve. But let me ask you again—what are the Feranmuts, in your eyes?
What are the proxies who live amongst us within the season, on whom we keep a constant watch?
The hearts of people are fickle; the hearts of beasts even more so. Perhaps some amongst Them have once aided Yan, be it out of a whim, or true sincerity.
But weighing the pros and cons, ultimately... They are different from us. They are... enemies.
Enemies, you say...
...Enemies.

lines.loc: One day before the day of the full moon, Hour of Yu

lines.x: Supervisor...
What are you doing here?
That question should be mine to ask.
Where have you taken the token from the Garden of Grotesqueries after the Placation was completed?
Something was not right at the Sui Regulator, and scriptgeists were running amok in the Sky Mirror Pavilion. I wanted to wait for the situation to settle down before seeking your counsel...
Then you can give it to me here.
I have heard that it was not the usual jade rule that was retrieved from the Garden of Grotesqueries this year. I will bring it back to the Sui Regulator for further investigation.
Acting on a subconscious instinct, Zuo Le finds himself taking a step back from his familiar superior.
My, my. I did not think there would come a day when Candleholder Zuo defied his orders.
Cut the crap—you're Chun, head of the Sui Regulator?
A Shanhaizhong member, in the Sky Mirror Pavilion. A highly ranked one, too, judging from your attire. Under normal circumstances, the Patrol Battalion would have faced severe penalties.
And this is... Lady Ch'en?
You're Zuo Le's boss?
Though I am unworthy of this position, it is my duty.
What are you doing here? Waiting for us to walk into a trap?
Unfortunately, it is not you that I am waiting for.
Hand over the token, Zuo Le, and you may leave with your friends. We can discuss your association with undesirable elements later.
I thought you would tell him to take me in on the spot to make up for his transgressions.
We are in the courts of Yan, not some bandit camp.
Fine enough for you to say.
Who was it who encouraged Jie to enter the Tomb of Sui, so that the more dangerous proxy for Yan would die in Wang's place, catapulting her own career in the process?
Yaan would be ashamed to have a descendant like you!
Mo Yi! The Supervisor is not like that!
Really? Then why did she become the Supervisor so soon after Jie's death?
Only a thief sees thieves everywhere.
Keep your mouth shut, for you are not worthy to speak her name.
The imperial court of Yan does not pause or waver for any person or thing. All that it does is measured against the interests of Great Yan.
The interests of Great Yan?
It seems Lady Ch'en has something to say.
Very well. I will give you three minutes, in consideration of the risks that Zuo Le ran to retrieve this object from the Garden of Grotesqueries.
There is someone I am waiting for anyway. Ask your questions, then leave the token and leave this place.
Waiting for someone?
Yes, waiting for someone.
Is it the proxy who holds the key?
Why do you say that?
Geists run rampant through Baizao, but Zuo Le is the only one who is trying to tackle the scriptgeists at the source, and on no orders at that.
The L.G.D. was never so negligent, even when Wei Yenwu's oversight was at its laxest.
The first thing you asked about when you saw Zuo Le was not the scriptgeists, but the token from the Garden of Grotesqueries... you seem to care nothing for your responsibilities.
The Sui Regulator is not the police.
Who in Baizao is more qualified than the Sui Regulator to deal with the scriptgeists?
Your goals are aligned with the Grand Commandant's... you want the writing knife.
And if Mo Yi is telling the truth, no one in Baizao is closer to the writing knife than Jun.
You do the L.G.D. proud, superintendent.
Why are you assisting the Grand Commandant's plot? What do you plan to do with Wei Yenwu? Or have you already joined forces with him?
Plot?
You may have been an excellent officer of the law, Lady Ch'en, but it is not your place to intervene in matters of national interest.
I've been to places and seen things. There's no need for grandiose words.
If your plot is indeed in the interest of the nation, you should feel no shame in saying it out loud.
Only those involved in political squabbles would keep bringing up 'national interest' when questioned, beating around the bush and deflecting the question.

You should be careful with your words, Lady Ch'en. The matters of state are complex and interconnected, and it is not unusual for officials to have differing views. Your words insult all who serve the state.
For a thousand years, the Sui Regulator has had one mission, and one alone—to do what is best for Yan in matters pertaining to Feranmuts. Nothing else matters to us.
Even if the Grand Commandant's actions are indeed drastic, extraordinary times call for extraordinary measures. The use of the writing knife does not constitute treason.
Then what is he planning—
At this point, I doubt this damned old Elf is gonna talk, so I'll do the talking for her.
Just as I suspected—the Grand Commandant wants to rewrite history with the writing knife and completely eliminate the bestial Sui. The price is the True Lung's life.
But why... what does the True Lung's life have to do with destroying a Feranmut?

We were just talking about it, before we were interrupted... a thousand years ago, the grandmaster discovered a piece of Originium that he presented to the True Lung as a weapon that can end the Great Hunt.
Realizing its importance, the True Lung had his Fangshi perform a ritual that linked it to True Lung's bloodline.
Henceforth, the power of the Originium may only be harnessed by the reigning True Lung.
That True Lung had a bit of a conscience, at least, and he set a condition in the ritual, probably to prevent his descendants from abusing it—
The full power of the Originium can only be unleashed by sacrificing the life of the True Lung. Hence it was given the name Bu Fan—'No Rebellion'.

But does the True Lung's life to be sacrificed really have to be the emperor's? Why did the Grand Commandant go to such painstaking lengths to obtain this writing knife, if his goal was merely a coup?
You mean... the Grand Commandant wants to use the writing knife to change the record of who the current True Lung is?!
'The Grand Preceptor incited the prince to regicide.'
What is the truth of what happened that night? Who sent the Yulin Guard away? Who actually entered the emperor's chambers? These things are not specified in the records...
Therefore, the records of what happened that night can still be rewritten using the writing knife.
If the history books and Bu Fan recognize Lord Wei as the reigning True Lung, then the life of the True Lung obviously refers to Lord Wei's life.
I had thought that you were young, and that though you may have strayed from the righteous path, you should be given the chance to right your wrongs.
Regrettably, it seems I cannot allow you to live.
Not putting on a show of honesty and uprightness anymore, are we?
Supervisor!
Hand over the token, Zuo Le! Whatever happens after that is none of your concern!
If what Mo Yi says is true... then I voice my dissent to your methods, Supervisor.
I cannot comply.
I don't want to damage the Sky Mirror Pavilion... don't make me do this.
You're talking to the wrong person. I have the token—
Ch'en's vision is obstructed by several maple leaves. The lamplight sways slightly.
Ch'en steps back and prepares to engage, only for Mo Yi to step forward and block Chun's attack.
So the Supervisor of the Sui Regulator is not above sneak attacks... like a thief.
One can be subtle and reserved with words, but action must be decisive and without hesitation. It is as true in weiqi as it is with all things in life.
Supervisor...
I have read about the writing knife in the past. In Jie's hands, it can indeed alter history and greatly influence events.
But we are not proxies. Our tampered historical records will never endure for long!
An instant is all it takes to activate Bu Fan.
Besides...
After this, there will no longer be a disgraced deposed crown prince in Yan, only a pure True Lung whose legitimacy is beyond question—is that right?
That is not the Sui Regulator's concern.
Since her death, Great Yan's history is already in tatters. One more change will hardly make a difference—
How can you be so sure that we cannot record history truthfully?
Ch'en Chao-ch'ien... Magistrate of Water for the Sky Mirror Pavilion.
I wanted to ask you as well, Supervisor...
For many years, you assisted Jie in compiling history in her office... why is it that you have now turned to tampering with history?
The records of Yan are classified into four parts: Classics, History, Philosophy, and Literature.
The divisions of History and Philosophy may seem distinct, but even the masterwork of the one venerated as the Saint of History was, merely the personal treatise within Philosophy.
Those who follow, modeling their brushes after his style, produce what is ultimately but the statement of a single house, and the House of Yan.
Those writings are no more than comments after the fact. The truth has long faded into the cracks of time.
How could you say that?
You must have read the Saint of History's notes. Despite imprisonment and torture, he always wrote the facts as they were and exposed injustice.

You must have read the history tomes of the Sky Mirror Pavilion, written by thousands of sages who poured their hearts and souls into them, striving to set the record straight, to weigh every word in judgment of right and wrong.

You must have heard of unofficial historians—meticulously investigating and analyzing their sources, coveting not the court's wealth and rank, but striving only to uphold justice for the common people.
I saw with my own eyes the elders of my family dying on white fields of snow because they wanted to write down the truth... nay, because they were honest to themselves!
These people, who wrote history with their own flesh and blood, who willingly became the flame to illuminate the truth even for a moment—
Who are they to you, Supervisor?
They are the writers.
True and false, right and wrong, these things always depend on the stance of the writer, rather than on what is actually right.
You ought to know—even with the revisions of countless esteemed scholars, the histories we read are still rife with embellishment, omissions and lies.
The ironic thing is... we could not even call such things 'evil'.
The curled leaves make a sharp sound against each other, questioningly, lamentingly.
Stand back, Ch'en Chao-ch'ien. You are neither Candleholder, nor Shanhaizhong, nor the niece of a deposed crown prince. This is not your fight.
I—
Did the Imperial Historical Cabinet truly take up responsibility for writing history after her passing? Did the Sky Mirror Pavilion truly fulfill its duty of righting the wrongs?
You are the descendant of historians. If you take up their mantle, can you truly guarantee that every word will be true for all eternity?
No. You dare not shoulder this responsibility.
I know about you.

You wanted to change the Sky Mirror Pavilion's culture of obsession with the analysis of ancient history while neglecting recent history. That's why you took up a minor position at the Sky Mirror Pavilion, hoping to lead by example by studying your family's history.
Decades, centuries, millennia. How could you claim to speak for history, when you have not borne witness to time on the scale that she knew?
Stand back.
The maple leaves sharpen.
This time, no one saw what Chun did. All she did was wave her sleeve; the next instant, Ch'en Chao-ch'ien was lying unconscious on the floor.
Chao-ch'ien?!
Supervisor—

She's just unconscious. She'll wake up in a couple of hours. There is no need for her to sink any deeper into this mire. She's but an unfortunate ordinary woman. She shouldn't have to be like you... or like me.
I hear Elves live a long time.
We do.
You don't believe in history or in lasting justice in people's hearts... because the one who had walked side-by-side with you on the long road of time died because of you?
This question is not yours to ask, Ch'en Hui-chieh.
As an Elf, you don't know where you came from, whether there are any of your kind left, or whether there'll ever be a home for you—
My past, present and future belong to Yan.
And that involves tossing your fellows into the abyss?
Having her die in Wang's place was the appropriate decision under the circumstances! This is beyond all doubt!
Compiling history, recording the passage of time... have you any idea of the power of these 'years', the power of time?

'The beast walks with the generous earth.' She had gone one step further. Her power was rooted in words, and lived in history. As long as the records of Yan continued, her power would continue to grow without end.
If left unchecked, what sort of monster would be created in the end? It would be something even more powerful, even more terrible than Sui itself!
She was my mentor, my confidant, my friend. But I could not endanger Great Yan with my own selfish interests.
I've seen people like you... people who abandoned their friends, but long to see them again in dreams, in order to beg for forgiveness.
Head of the Sui Regulator, what would you do if there was a way to see her again?
I warn you, do not play games with the lives of millions for the fantasies of a madman!
Wang did not go into the Tomb of Sui for the sole purpose of eliminating Sui.
The object that he had you retrieve from the Garden of Grotesqueries surely has something to do with his deranged plans to bring the dead back to life.
I'm surprised... I thought you got along well with her when you were her Candleholder.
Isn't there a part of you, somewhere, that wants his plan to succeed?
He already failed a hundred and twenty years ago. He will fail again. Allowing him to continue on his reckless path will only lead to more disaster.
How can I trust a madman who treats heaven and earth as his weiqi board, and all lives and souls as his pieces? How can I trust a sinner who caused the death of his sister?
How can I trust the obsession of a Feranmut?
Because, like you, he walked this land, lived in the seasons, and vowed to become 'human', to become part of this land and those who live upon it.
'Human'?
Humans are humans because they live and die. This is why they shine, as the candle's fleeting glow.
She has lived, and she has died. Wang seeks to disturb the slumber of the dead, in defiance of the laws of Heaven.
Even if his delusions somehow came true, how can you be sure that it is the departed herself who has returned and not a geist, a hollow puppet that only carries her image?
You just don't want to believe.
Things seldom go the way people want them to.
The outcome may not be exactly what we want. But to be shackled by regret and not even have the courage to take a single step...
...That is nothing but cowardice.
You have a way with words, Ch'en Hui-chieh. But I don't see how you intend to carry out the justice you speak of.
The token and the key. I am only here for them.
Continue to play games with words, and I will crush you before dealing with them.
Let's see who's crushing who.
What about you, Zuo Le?
I apologize, but I must cause offense.
Hah. So be it.
Those who stay near ink get stained. You have made some good friends, Candleholder Zuo.
Do your worst.
You almost hit a bookshelf.
I am no swordswoman and I have no interest in admiring Chi Xiao. But it would be a shame if your sword ruins the collection of the Sky Mirror Pavilion.
But you are not confident about taking the token from me.
Is that so?
The Elf now truly waves her jade scepter and twig for the first time.
Withered vines rush out from the ground, forming massive hands.
Dammit, she's a nightmare to deal with! Is the Sui Regulator's Supervisor decided through combat?!

How does the book go anyway... ah, I remember—and it's autumn, this is perfect...
'Metal's nature is change.'
Take this!
For a thief, you're pretty well-learned.
I wouldn't be surprised if I know Yin-Yang and the Five Elements better than someone who's forgotten her roots.
Zuo Le! Ch'en Hui-chieh! Your turn!
Zuo Le charges towards Chun, intent on disrupting her ability to respond.
Meanwhile, Ch'en Hui-chieh pulls back, waiting for an opportunity to strike.
In between them, Mo Yi unleashes razor-sharp autumn winds, tangling with the vine hands.
The Supervisor of the Sui Regulator continues to stand where she is, utterly impassive, as though she is watching a farce that she is becoming increasingly bored with.
Ch'en Hui-chieh continues to back off, until her back is almost touching the closed door.
Damn... can't hold on much longer!
Ch'en!
Chi Xiao...

lines.st: Splits the clouds—

lines.x: Riding on the autumn wind, the sword aura sweeps away all the vines. The Elf looks surprised for a brief moment.
Then, dried leaves sharp as razors fly out from the vines towards the trio.
Mo Yi urgently casts a defensive spell, but the leaves cut their clothes and bags open...
The token that has slumbered since it was retrieved from the Garden of Grotesqueries falls on the ground.
This is the token from the Garden of Grotesqueries?
It is a geist.
It is, indeed, a geist.
The weiqi player had taken Sui's wildest imagination from the Garden of Grotesqueries and hollowed out the interior, leaving only the gleaming exterior...
The memories of his siblings were then injected to create this object.
Made of a glass-like substance, the tiny geist lies immobile on the ground for a while... then, it seems to grow ethereal.
It awakens.
It does not seem to feel pain. Or perhaps, the pain was overcome by the urge to return to this familiar place.
She begins to stumble towards the door.
That is...
Impossible!
All it takes is a single thought from the Elf, and the churning vines could shatter the object to smithereens. But Chun cannot bring herself to do it.
Because she truly does see a familiar figure in it.
The object touches the door, and it is as though a dream that has slumbered for a hundred years is suddenly awakened by an intruder.
The falling dust forms thousands of golden threads drifting in the air in the setting sun. The sliver of life force ripples.
All breaths are held as they see the ethereal figure that drifts between light and shadow in the falling dust.
It is blurry, indistinct, the only clear feature its grey robe, like paper that has grown yellow from the passage of time, but still smelling faintly of old books and dried ink...
The door opens slowly.
How do you get in, if you give me the key?
Why should the master of the house need a key?
The shadow bends slightly, as though trying to pick up the cracked scroll on the desk.
Or perhaps it is walking towards the window to open the sash closed for so long, to see if the osmanthus that was young back then has bloomed this year.
But her movement ultimately freezes between the done and the undone, a faint wisp of melancholy suspended in the sunset.
Put this scroll on the shelf by the wall. Leave that document on the desk; it's not yet finished.
A painting from Dusk? I should find a good place to hang it.
Why do I have to do these chores for you?!
Oh? But Nian said her Candleholder even helps her put together mahjong games.
Desk, chair and scrolls... every object in the room breathes with the shadow. They are her old friends, and she is the reason they never left.
A gust of wind blows through the room. The geist falls asleep and the shadow dissipates like a drop of ink that falls into water, leaving behind only that which is real, the shadows of dust.
And the sun, shining into an empty room.
It's...
Her...
No, she was a little taller, her hair a little blacker... she was thin, but not like that...
She cannot, would not accept, yet there is a single question that she could never answer.
Who else could it be?
Who else could it be?
The stunned Elf does not even notice the appearance of the proxy, one who is very much flesh and blood.
She continues to stand at the same spot, staring in the direction where the shadow disappeared.
I came as you asked.
But it seems you no longer have anything to say to me.
It's her... it's really her...
I never thought... I didn't think there was any trace of her existence left...
There is no doubt about it. It's a sliver of her life force, which those young people retrieved from the Sui's Remnant Consciousness.
As for traces of her existence... they are in the books of the Sky Mirror Pavilion, in countless scholars across the land of Yan.
Now, do you still believe that everything we siblings have done since Jie's disappearance is futile, Candleholder Chun?
If... if you can still find a trace of her somewhere...
Tell her...
The long-lived Candleholder chokes on her own words. She gazes wordlessly at the study, the interior of which has not changed for a century.
Having taken it all in, she turns around and slowly walks towards the exit, fighting the urge to look back at every step.
Leaving this place she knows best, yet brings her the most despair.
...I did not know that Wang's obsession was this paranoid, yet so deep.
I saw you at the city gates...
And we meet again.
You are... Jun?
Thank you for protecting her token.
Lady Jun... no, Master Jun.
I heard you were a close friend of the grandmaster's, and I have long heard your name spoken of with reference... it is an honor to meet you.
Don't try to curry favor with me. I'm not even sure if I had been born at the time of the old carpenter's death.
I'm glad to see that someone still remembers his views after a thousand years have passed.
But did you do what you did at Mangshan Town out of a desire for peace, or out of your own bitter cynicism? Do you really need me to tell you?
The old Tianshi gave you an opportunity to make up for your transgressions. Have you done so?
The proxy does not raise her voice, and there is no anger in it, but her voice naturally begets respect.
I've long heard of the second Sui sister's unapproachable nature. Today, I got to witness it.
There is always hope for those who learn from their mistakes.
Zuo Le.
Academician Jun...
Words cannot express my gratitude for you bringing back this sliver of Jie's life force from the Garden of Grotesqueries.
Our family will remember the favor, and repay it when the time is right.
I'm simply doing my duty as a Candleholder. The Supervisor lost her composure, but the candle of the Sui Regulator remains lit.

She was trapped between the greater good and personal desire, and was eventually crushed by her own obsessions... you must not repeat her mistakes, when the time comes for you to take on great responsibility.
Now, though, it is more useful for you to go outside than to stay here and look at relics of the past.
Take this girl too, and put her somewhere safe downstairs. It is late autumn, and the floor is cold.
I trust you will handle the matter of the writing knife properly, Academician Jun.
Of course.
One more request, if I may.
Jun turns around and picks up the geist from the ground.
She has turned back into the small, quiet doll, perhaps exhausted from the action she just performed.
You know that this geist was formed by the last speck of Jie's life force.
Wang drew you into the game and had you retrieve this object so that you could deliver it here and allow it to recuperate, in preparation for her return.
But... consider it a burst of sentimentality from a reunion with someone long thought lost.
Please deliver it to the Tomb of Sui. The future is yet unsettled. Our family will gather outside the tomb.
I do not know if the day will come when Jie returns. But if Wang sees it as Jie's foundation, she should be there for this meeting.
Wang is in the tomb, and I must help him. There will be a battle. Carrying this object would be unsafe, and perhaps cause a delay...
Leave it to me!
The Shanhaizhong snatches the token from the woman's hands before the Candleholder can react.
I will see it done, in the name of Zushi!
The dignified woman bows deeply to the two youngsters.
Thank you.
Lady Jun...
You blocked the attack against me at the gate, and protected Jie's legacy.
Has chivalry become a habit of yours?
I do what I do for the sake of justice.
What is justice?
Justice is making sure that Wei Yenwu does not die an unjust death, and giving the old case a proper trial.
Justice is bringing closure to all that has happened between mother and father, between the imperial family and the House of Ch'en.
Justice is making sure that the proxy who did so much for Great Yan did not die in vain, and giving Great Yan a chance at peace.
What about justice for you as an Infected?
I have seen so much suffering in my journeys... this land has been too kind to me. I wield my justice for the sake of others.
Very well.
I can't help you with the justice you seek. But perhaps her relic can.
Do you mean... the writing knife?!
Take it.
The justice you seek is closely linked to the incident forty years ago.
You are the only one to whom I can entrust this knife, who can right the wrongs for your family, for your country.
There should be closure to the matters of the Ch'en family, Lord Wei's decision, and the thousand years of records for the land on which she once lived and breathed.
These were the hopes that she placed upon the writing knife.
The history of Great Yan should be written by the Yanese people.
Come with me.
They walk into the room.
Like revisiting a scene from an old play, Jun slowly walks through the room that has not changed one bit in the past century.
Everything is familiar. No one has touched it for a hundred years.
It's been a while.
The writing knife has resurfaced, but it is no longer in your hands. If I have entrusted it to the wrong person...
I will shoulder the responsibility for any consequences on your behalf.
Ch'en's palm closes around the knife. Her fingertip trembles, as if the weight of a thousand books is upon her heart.
She has given her all wholeheartedly, and sacrificed herself selflessly. Now, everything has come together as appointed.
She clutches the writing knife. In her eyes, something sinks, and something else burns bright.
—It is the weight of an era, and a reunion that the knife has long awaited.
I hope... you will not disappoint.
...I will not disappoint.
What's going on?!
These geists just appeared out of nowhere!
The annals of Mangshan Town record that similar scenes of rampaging geists were spotted during the turmoil caused by Sui a hundred and twenty years ago.
Is the bestial Sui awakening?
What a headache... guess it's happened after all.
Number Two, you fool, why are you dilly-dallying? How many times do I have to bail you out?
Lin Qingyan!
What are your instructions?
This is where the geists are most rampant. They must not be allowed to enter Baizao!
You and I will guard this pass. Do not let a single geist through!
Yes, master!
I can't hear you, girl!
Look lively! Treat this as your entrance exam. Pass the test, and I'll take you as my student. Then you can make your old teacher call you 'master'!
I wouldn't dare!
Is there anything that I can help with?
Help? What could you do...
Watch my moves and record them. Then you can show them to your teacher and see who's better.
The fire rises—
Wait... *pant* wait for me... *pant*
You run too fast!
What's the matter? You were the one who was in a hurry.
If you're out of breath, give it to me and I'll deliver it.
In your dreams!
You blockhead! Did you think about how we're supposed to enter the Tomb of Sui when we arrive?
We'll explain to the soldiers guarding the tomb and ask them to let us through...
Riiiiggghhht. And how do you plan to explain where you got the thing you're holding?
Or... maybe find a way to sneak in...
Your brain really is made entirely of wood, isn't it?
Do you have an idea, then?
If we can't get inside the Tomb of Sui, we'll find someone who can.

Do you mean...
Who's there—
Show yourself!
Zuo Le senses a hostile presence and instinctively draws his sword.
The thing you promised me.
Hand it over.
The Forbidden City is just ahead...
It should be heavily guarded, if the Grand Commandant really intends to sacrifice Wei Yenwu's life.
There are guards stationed at all the important intersections in the city. It should not be hard to detect unusual movements by a large force.
Wait, is that...
Ch'en stops in her tracks.
She sees a figure.
A very familiar one.
Hui-chieh.
You should not have come.
There's no end to them...
Look out!
Where did these monsters even come from, and just how many of them are there?!
They don't even seem to be decreasing... in fact, there's more of them now.
I heard last night, the third battalion in the north lost half their numbers...
What?!
Have you noticed? I don't know when it started, but these mysterious monsters are looking even more realistic than they did, and more aggressive to boot...
Where's our ta ma de backup?!
We're in dire straits, if this keeps up—
Stand up.
We must uphold our duty to guard the land.
Imperial Guards?!
All units, form a line and prepare to meet the enemy.
There is no retreat until we annihilate all evil inside the city.
Your Imperial Majesty.
The Grand Commandant's sickness seems to be gone... this gladdens me.
What is it that has you looking for me at this hour?
Has Your Imperial Majesty forgotten? Just the other day, You commanded me to be here for the morning court.
I fear you must have slept to delirium... court is long over. You are late.
No, not yet... the day of the full moon is tomorrow.
This old servant shall accompany Your Majesty and await your order.
...I find myself uncertain why the Grand Commandant wants this done in haste.
I know not what Your Majesty awaits.
And also... what Your Majesty has planned for the day.
That is none of your business.
Your Majesty's silence only keeps this servant guessing.
Would Your Imperial Majesty truly risk your sacred person to activate Bu Fan to settle the crisis within the Tomb of Sui?
I know not whose slander Your Majesty has heard... to resort to such an ill-advised course.
With no counsel offered, am I not capable of making my own decision?
Forty years ago... when Your Majesty ascended the throne, it was the Grand Tutor and I who went with you to survey Bu Fan.
I still remember following our visit to that remote room, three whole days came and went without a single court session. To this day I remain unaware of Your Majesty's thoughts at the time.
I remember then as well... But now I no longer fear the Grand Commandant deriding me, so perhaps I shall share.
I was only thinking... in Yan's thousand year history with this object, not even once has there been a single True Lung who used it.
If Great Yan were forced to use Bu Fan while I sat the throne... would I not become the weakest ruler in our history?
If you would grant me a presumptuous question... If Your Imperial Majesty were to sacrifice yourself for the nation, how would it be run, subsequently?
Is there a cause for worry...? Is my replacement not already in Baizao as we speak?
The mandarins would be unsatisfied if the deposed imperial heir Yan Wu were to take the throne, and the country would descend into chaos. And what will happen then?
The fault will lie on the mandarins' shoulders.
I only wish for Your Majesty to abandon this thought.
Ridiculous...
You know well I am no match for the late emperor, whom you so admired...
Why not accept and anticipate my brother's imminent ascension to the throne, where he will be able to realize your ambitions...?
Why trouble yourself to persuade me?
Your Majesty!
The old man suddenly tenses up, his trembling voice nearly hoarse.
The man on the throne seems to have forgotten when he last saw the old man this agitated.
For the past forty years, I have witnessed Your Majesty toil tirelessly to grow our country... not even once did you slacken from your imperial duties.
In those years, Great Yan has enjoyed stability. Our population swelled, and the treasury remains full.
Has there been an era in our history... even more prosperous than this one... Or an emperor... more capable and benevolent than the one we have now?
And yet, Your Majesty... do you intend to idly watch as our nation's golden age...
Vanishes into night during this turbulent time?
An urgent message from the Sui Regulator. A strong energy wave has been reported from the depths of the Tomb of Sui.
Far stronger than the wave a hundred and twenty years ago.
And how fare the troops and civilians?
Under control at the moment. Baizao's peripheral blocks moved away before the energy blast, and non-mobile settlements nearby have also been largely evacuated. The damage is minor.
Mt. Mang has suffered more damage, but the Tianshis are at their posts, and the Garden of Grotesqueries is still suppressing Sui.
There are geists running amok in the city, and the Imperial Guards are cooperating with the Patrol Battalion to establish a defense line. They will give it their all.
I see... You may go.
From behind the curtain, the True Lung rises from his throne, and descends the steps until he is next to the older man.
It's about time.
You may withdraw, Grand Commandant. The battlefronts of Baizao and Yumen await your commands.
Your Imperial Majesty...
For the sake of Yan... Is Your Majesty truly unaware why I so strongly advocate for war?
Do you still remember why the court came up with the Twenty-Eight Policies?
Victoria, Leithanien, Kazdel... this whole great land is plagued by unrest, Great Yan aside.
We are now in terrible peril... and the nation cannot afford to be passive. We must proactively seek change...
Has Your Majesty not learned from what happened in Ursus?!
The Chamber of Heaven's Designs has yet to figure out what sort of power managed to wipe out that entire Originium vein.
If the Crystal Age truly ends with the depletion of every prime vein, then Great Yan must take the responsibility and prepare to lead all of Terra.

Attack Ursus in the north, repel Victoria in the west, pacify Mitsukue and Kusarigawa in the east, and settle Rim Billiton to the south. Furthermore, we must keep a close eye on Columbia's developments as well as possible naval incursions from Ægir's own sea.

And before all of that... while Great Yan stands at the height of its power, we must seize the chance in this fight against the bestial Sui to sweep away decades of weakness hiding under peace and prosperity!
Only then... will we manage to stand tall in this chaotic realm... undeterred for the next millennium.
The True Lung falls silent at the old man's speech. Not only to consider all that was said, but also to calm his own mind.
No one knows how much time has passed before he finally speaks.
You dedicated your life to wars for the country, and your military merit is without equal, but this has also left you with injuries and ailments...
Have I ever doubted your undying loyalty to the country?
But earlier, you spoke of what befell Ursus, and that makes me wonder...
Do you remember... what happened to Gaul?
Your Imperial Majesty...?
Your words were incisive...
Yes, Great Yan should seek change, and must change...
But the way I see it, compared to the lives we may lose to this war with the bestial Sui...
